# TUI interaction and rendering tests  `stage-23.5`

This stage is the safety net for the terminal user interface, or TUI: the text-based app people see and interact with. It mostly belongs to the system’s main working life, checking that screens, prompts, menus, and status lines behave correctly while the app is running, and that startup and shutdown details still look right.

A few shared helpers make these tests possible. `test_support.rs`, `app/test_support.rs`, and the chat widget helpers build realistic fake app state so tests stay short but believable. `test_backend.rs` provides a pretend terminal powered by a VT100 parser, so tests can “look at the screen” without using a real terminal window. The integration entry files under `tui/tests/` gather everything into one test program.

From there, the tests cover the app from different angles. App-level files check startup routing, session summaries, config updates, shutdown, and cross-module behavior. Chat widget tests are the biggest group: they verify message submission, slash commands, approvals, popups, permissions, review and plan modes, layout, status surfaces, terminal title updates, and replaying old sessions. Other files lock down rendering details for history cells, markdown, token charts, status output, and layout math, plus a few smoke tests that drive the real TUI.

## Files in this stage

### Shared TUI test infrastructure
These files define the common test harnesses, backend shims, and aggregate integration-test entry points that the rest of the TUI test suites build on.

### `tui/src/test_support.rs`

`test` · `test setup`

This file provides reusable fixtures and conversion helpers for tests. Its largest piece of state is `TEST_MODEL_PRESETS`, a `LazyLock<Vec<ModelPreset>>` initialized from `codex_models_manager::bundled_models_response()`. Initialization sorts bundled models by `priority`, converts them into `ModelPreset`, and then calls `ModelPreset::mark_default_by_picker_visibility` so tests see the same default-selection behavior as production picker code.

The exported path helpers re-export `PathBufExt` and `test_path_buf`, and `test_path_display` turns a test-relative path string into a displayable string using the same path-building utility. The remaining helpers bridge between app-server protocol enums and whatever deserializable type a test wants to compare against. `session_source_cli`, `skill_scope_user`, and `skill_scope_repo` each call the private generic `from_app_server_wire`, which serializes a protocol value to `serde_json::Value` and immediately deserializes it into `T`. That round-trip intentionally avoids hand-written mapping code and panics with a descriptive message if the wire representation no longer matches the legacy helper type expected by tests. The design is deliberately test-centric: fail fast, keep fixtures canonical, and avoid duplicating protocol enum definitions in test code.

#### Function details

##### `test_path_display`  (lines 21–23)

```
fn test_path_display(path: &str) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Builds a normalized display string for a test path. It is a convenience wrapper so tests can compare rendered paths without manually constructing `PathBuf`s.

**Data flow**: Takes `path: &str`, passes it to `test_path_buf(path)`, then converts the resulting path to a display string with `.display().to_string()`, returning that `String`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need stable path text. It delegates path construction to the shared absolute-path test helper.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (test_path_buf).


##### `session_source_cli`  (lines 25–30)

```
fn session_source_cli() -> T
```

**Purpose**: Produces a caller-chosen deserializable representation of the app-server `SessionSource::Cli` value. It lets tests obtain legacy helper types from the canonical wire enum.

**Data flow**: Has no runtime inputs beyond the target type `T: DeserializeOwned`. It passes `codex_app_server_protocol::SessionSource::Cli` into `from_app_server_wire` and returns the deserialized `T`.

**Call relations**: Called by session telemetry tests that need a CLI session-source value in another type shape. It delegates all conversion work to `from_app_server_wire`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_app_server_wire); called by 2 (test_session_telemetry, test_session_telemetry).


##### `skill_scope_user`  (lines 32–37)

```
fn skill_scope_user() -> T
```

**Purpose**: Produces a caller-chosen deserializable representation of the app-server `SkillScope::User` value. It keeps tests aligned with the wire enum while avoiding bespoke conversion code.

**Data flow**: Uses the generic target type `T: DeserializeOwned`, serializes `codex_app_server_protocol::SkillScope::User` through `from_app_server_wire`, and returns the resulting `T`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need a user-scoped skill value in a legacy/helper type. It is a thin wrapper over `from_app_server_wire`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_app_server_wire).


##### `skill_scope_repo`  (lines 39–44)

```
fn skill_scope_repo() -> T
```

**Purpose**: Produces a caller-chosen deserializable representation of the app-server `SkillScope::Repo` value. It mirrors `skill_scope_user` for repository scope.

**Data flow**: Uses the generic target type `T: DeserializeOwned`, passes `codex_app_server_protocol::SkillScope::Repo` into `from_app_server_wire`, and returns the deserialized `T`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need repo-scoped skill values. It delegates conversion to `from_app_server_wire`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_app_server_wire).


##### `from_app_server_wire`  (lines 46–55)

```
fn from_app_server_wire(value: impl Serialize) -> T
```

**Purpose**: Converts any serializable app-server wire value into an arbitrary deserializable target type by round-tripping through JSON. It is a compatibility shim for tests that compare protocol values against older helper structs or enums.

**Data flow**: Takes `value: impl Serialize`, serializes it with `serde_json::to_value`, immediately deserializes that `Value` with `serde_json::from_value` into `T: DeserializeOwned`, and returns `T`. On any serialization/deserialization mismatch it panics with a message explaining that the app-server wire value should map to the legacy helper type.

**Call relations**: This private helper underpins `session_source_cli`, `skill_scope_user`, and `skill_scope_repo`. Those wrappers supply concrete protocol enum values; this function performs the generic transformation and enforces fail-fast behavior.

*Call graph*: called by 3 (session_source_cli, skill_scope_repo, skill_scope_user); 1 external calls (to_value).


### `tui/src/test_backend.rs`

`test` · `test rendering`

This file defines `VT100Backend`, a thin wrapper around `ratatui::prelude::CrosstermBackend<vt100::Parser>`. Its purpose is to behave enough like a real terminal backend for Ratatui widgets while avoiding crossterm operations that bypass the configured writer and talk directly to stdout, especially terminal-size and cursor-position queries. Tests can therefore render full screens deterministically and inspect the resulting virtual terminal state.

`VT100Backend::new` forces color output on and constructs a `vt100::Parser` with the requested height and width, then wraps it in `CrosstermBackend`. The helper `vt100()` exposes the underlying parser for read-only inspection. The type implements `std::io::Write` by forwarding bytes and flushes to the parser-backed writer, and implements `fmt::Display` by returning the parser screen contents as plain text for snapshots.

Its `Backend` implementation mostly delegates drawing, cursor visibility, clearing, scrolling, and cursor movement to the wrapped crossterm backend. The notable exceptions are `get_cursor_position`, `size`, and `window_size`, which read directly from the parser’s screen state instead of querying the host terminal. `window_size` fabricates a fixed pixel size because tests only care about character dimensions. This design keeps rendering behavior close to production while making tests hermetic and inspectable.

#### Function details

##### `VT100Backend::new`  (lines 27–32)

```
fn new(width: u16, height: u16) -> Self
```

**Purpose**: Constructs a virtual terminal backend with a parser-backed screen of the requested dimensions. It also forces color output so ANSI styling is preserved during tests.

**Data flow**: Consumes `width` and `height`, calls `crossterm::style::force_color_output(true)`, creates a `vt100::Parser::new(height, width, 0)`, wraps it in `CrosstermBackend::new`, and returns a `VT100Backend` holding that backend.

**Call relations**: Many snapshot-style UI tests instantiate this backend before rendering widgets or full app frames. It is the entry constructor for the rest of the methods in this file and delegates setup to external parser/backend constructors.

*Call graph*: called by 61 (thread_goal_ephemeral_error_message_renders_snapshot, render_footer_with_mode_indicator_and_context, chained_config_error_wraps_in_history_snapshot, approval_modal_exec_multiline_prefix_hides_execpolicy_option_snapshot, approval_modal_exec_snapshot, approval_modal_exec_without_reason_snapshot, approval_modal_patch_snapshot, app_server_guardian_review_denied_renders_denied_request_snapshot, app_server_guardian_review_timed_out_renders_timed_out_request_snapshot, guardian_approved_exec_renders_approved_request (+15 more)); 3 external calls (new, force_color_output, new).


##### `VT100Backend::vt100`  (lines 34–36)

```
fn vt100(&self) -> &vt100::Parser
```

**Purpose**: Exposes the underlying `vt100::Parser` for read-only inspection of screen state. Tests and internal backend methods use it to query cursor position and dimensions without host-terminal syscalls.

**Data flow**: Reads `self.crossterm_backend` and returns `&vt100::Parser` via the wrapped backend’s `writer()` accessor. It does not mutate state.

**Call relations**: This helper is used internally by `get_cursor_position`, `size`, and `window_size` to avoid crossterm’s stdout-based queries. It serves as the bridge from Ratatui backend wrapper to parser screen inspection.

*Call graph*: called by 3 (get_cursor_position, size, window_size); 1 external calls (writer).


##### `VT100Backend::write`  (lines 40–42)

```
fn write(&mut self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize>
```

**Purpose**: Implements byte writes for the backend by feeding terminal escape sequences and text into the parser-backed writer. This lets Ratatui and crossterm update the virtual screen exactly as if writing to a terminal stream.

**Data flow**: Takes `&[u8]` input, obtains `writer_mut()` from the wrapped backend, forwards the bytes to its `Write::write`, and returns the resulting `io::Result<usize>`.

**Call relations**: This method participates in the standard `Write` trait path used by terminal rendering machinery. It delegates directly to the wrapped writer and does not add logic of its own.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (writer_mut).


##### `VT100Backend::fmt`  (lines 50–52)

```
fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result
```

**Purpose**: Formats the backend as the current textual contents of the virtual terminal screen. It is primarily useful for assertions and snapshot output.

**Data flow**: Reads `self.crossterm_backend.writer().screen().contents()` and writes that string into the provided formatter, returning `fmt::Result`.

**Call relations**: Used implicitly when tests stringify the backend for diagnostics or snapshots. It depends on the parser-maintained screen contents rather than recomputing any rendering.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (write!).


##### `VT100Backend::draw`  (lines 56–62)

**Purpose**: Renders Ratatui cell content into the virtual terminal by forwarding the draw iterator to the wrapped crossterm backend. It preserves the backend trait contract while normalizing the return to `Ok(())` after delegation succeeds.

**Data flow**: Consumes an iterator of `(x, y, &Cell)` tuples, passes it to `self.crossterm_backend.draw(content)`, propagates any `io::Error`, and otherwise returns `Ok(())`.

**Call relations**: Called by Ratatui’s frame rendering pipeline during tests. It delegates the actual terminal diff/write behavior to the wrapped backend.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (draw).

```

**Purpose**: Issues the terminal command to show the cursor in the virtual backend. It mirrors production cursor visibility behavior for tests.

**Purpose**: Returns the cursor position from the parser’s tracked screen state instead of querying the real terminal. This avoids crossterm APIs that ignore the configured writer.

**Call relations**: Called by code using the `Backend` trait when it needs cursor coordinates during tests. Unlike most backend methods, it intentionally bypasses the wrapped crossterm backend and uses `vt100()` for hermetic behavior.

##### `VT100Backend::size`  (lines 94–97)

```
fn size(&self) -> io::Result<Size>
```

**Purpose**: Reports the terminal character dimensions from the parser’s screen state rather than the host terminal. This makes tests deterministic and independent of the machine running them.

**Data flow**: Reads `(rows, cols)` from `self.vt100().screen().size()`, constructs `Size::new(cols, rows)`, and returns it in `Ok(...)`.

**Call relations**: Called through the `Backend` trait when Ratatui needs terminal dimensions. Like `get_cursor_position`, it intentionally uses `vt100()` instead of crossterm’s real-terminal query path.

##### `VT100Backend::window_size`  (lines 99–108)

```
fn window_size(&mut self) -> io::Result<WindowSize>
```

**Purpose**: Returns a synthetic `WindowSize` based on the parser’s character dimensions plus a fixed pixel size. Tests only rely on columns/rows, so pixel dimensions are arbitrary placeholders.

**Data flow**: Reads screen size from `self.vt100().screen().size()`, converts it into `columns_rows`, fills `pixels` with `Size { width: 640, height: 480 }`, and returns the assembled `WindowSize` in `Ok(...)`.

**Call relations**: Used when code asks for full window metrics during tests. It combines parser-derived character size with hard-coded pixel values to avoid external dependencies.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (vt100).

### `tui/src/chatwidget/tests.rs`

`test` · `test setup and shared test utilities`

This file is the root of the `chatwidget` test tree. Most of its content is not executable logic but a curated wall of `pub(super) use` re-exports that make the many focused test modules compile without each one repeating the same imports. Those re-exports cover app commands/events, approval request types, protocol notifications, config and model types, terminal/test backends, helper utilities, and assertion crates. It also defines the `assert_chatwidget_snapshot!` macro, which standardizes snapshot naming and points `insta` at the dedicated `src/chatwidget/snapshots` directory so all submodule snapshots land in one predictable place.

The two actual functions are small but important test utilities. `chatwidget_snapshot_dir()` resolves the canonical snapshot directory by locating a known snapshot resource and taking its parent, failing loudly if the resource is missing or malformed. `next_goal_draft()` is an event-draining helper for tests that expect a `SetThreadGoalDraft` app event; it loops over `try_recv()` until it finds the right variant, asserts the thread id matches the expected one, and returns the embedded draft. The remainder of the file wires in the focused test submodules (`app_server`, `approval_requests`, `composer_submission`, and many others) and re-exports helper constructors such as `make_chatwidget_manual_with_sender`.

#### Function details

##### `chatwidget_snapshot_dir`  (lines 183–192)

```
fn chatwidget_snapshot_dir() -> PathBuf
```

**Purpose**: Finds the filesystem directory where `ChatWidget` snapshots are stored. It anchors the lookup on a known snapshot resource and returns that file’s parent directory.

**Data flow**: Calls `codex_utils_cargo_bin::find_resource!` with a specific snapshot path, expects the resource to exist, then takes `parent()` of the resulting path and converts it to `PathBuf` → panics if the resource is missing or has no parent.

**Call relations**: Used by the `assert_chatwidget_snapshot!` macro so all snapshot assertions in submodules write to and compare against the same directory.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (find_resource!).


##### `next_goal_draft`  (lines 217–231)

```
fn next_goal_draft(
    rx: &mut tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<AppEvent>,
    expected_thread_id: ThreadId,
) -> crate::goal_files::GoalDraft
```

**Purpose**: Pulls app events from a test receiver until it finds a `SetThreadGoalDraft` event for the expected thread, then returns the draft payload. It is a focused helper for tests that need to inspect emitted goal drafts.

**Data flow**: Consumes `rx` and `expected_thread_id`, repeatedly calls `rx.try_recv().expect(...)`, pattern-matches for `AppEvent::SetThreadGoalDraft { thread_id, draft, .. }`, asserts `thread_id == expected_thread_id`, and returns `draft` once found.

**Call relations**: Called by goal-related tests in submodules when they expect the widget to emit a goal-draft event and want to ignore unrelated events in the queue.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (try_recv, assert_eq!).


### `tui/src/chatwidget/tests/helpers.rs`

`test` · `test setup, synthetic event injection, snapshot/assertion rendering`

This file is the shared support layer for ChatWidget tests, and it is intentionally concrete rather than generic: most helpers either build a fully initialized `ChatWidget` in a deterministic temporary environment or inject exact `ServerNotification` / replay payloads that mimic app-server traffic. `test_config` creates a fresh temp-backed `Config` rooted under a synthetic codex home, resets warning/config-layer state, and pins `cwd` to `/tmp/project` via `test_path_display` so tests do not inherit host configuration. `make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth` then wires channels for `AppEvent` and `Op`, resolves a model, creates `SessionTelemetry`, installs a test `ModelCatalog`, and mutates several widget internals (`transcript.active_cell`, placeholder text, bottom pane placeholder, selected model) to a known baseline.

A large middle section is a protocol-event factory: helpers build token usage updates, turn lifecycle notifications, agent/user/reasoning items, review mode transitions, exec/apply-patch/image events, approval requests, hook runs, and replay snapshots. These functions consistently derive `thread_id` and default missing turn IDs to `"turn-1"`, which is an important invariant for tests that do not simulate full thread startup. There are also queue-draining and rendering helpers that convert `ratatui` buffers or history cells into plain strings, plus path and OSC-8 normalization so snapshots compare visible content rather than platform-specific paths or hyperlink escapes. The plugin helpers fabricate `PluginSummary`, `PluginDetail`, marketplace responses, and popup rendering inputs, letting tests exercise plugin menus without filesystem or network discovery.

#### Function details

##### `test_config`  (lines 5–23)

```
async fn test_config() -> Config
```

**Purpose**: Builds an isolated `Config` for tests from the application's default configuration logic, but rooted in a fresh temporary codex home. It then overwrites path fields and clears layered-config and startup-warning state so tests start from a deterministic baseline.

**Data flow**: It creates a temp directory, passes that path plus an empty CLI override list into `Config::load_default_with_cli_overrides_for_codex_home`, awaits the loaded config, then mutates `codex_home`, `sqlite_home`, `log_dir`, `cwd`, `config_layer_stack`, and `startup_warnings`. It returns the fully adjusted `Config` value.

**Call relations**: This is the configuration source for `make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth`, which uses it whenever a test needs a real widget instance without inheriting host machine settings.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth); 5 external calls (from, new, load_default_with_cli_overrides_for_codex_home, default, new).


##### `test_project_path`  (lines 25–27)

```
fn test_project_path() -> PathBuf
```

**Purpose**: Returns the canonical synthetic project path used throughout ChatWidget tests. It centralizes the `/tmp/project` test root behind `test_path_display` path translation.

**Data flow**: It takes no arguments, converts the normalized test path string into a `PathBuf`, and returns it.

**Call relations**: This is a standalone convenience helper for tests that need the same project root path used by `test_config` and snapshot normalization.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (from).


##### `truncated_path_variants`  (lines 29–34)

```
fn truncated_path_variants(path: &str) -> Vec<String>
```

**Purpose**: Generates every non-empty proper prefix of a path string as separate strings. Tests use these prefixes to normalize truncated path displays that end with an ellipsis.

**Data flow**: It reads the input `&str`, collects its chars, slices prefixes from length 1 up to `len - 1`, converts each prefix back into a `String`, and returns them in increasing-length order.

**Call relations**: It is only used by `normalize_snapshot_paths` when replacing platform-specific truncated path prefixes with the Unix-form prefixes expected by snapshots.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (normalize_snapshot_paths).


##### `normalize_snapshot_paths`  (lines 36–63)

```
fn normalize_snapshot_paths(text: impl Into<String>) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Rewrites platform-specific test paths in rendered text back to the canonical Unix snapshot forms. It also fixes truncated path prefixes like `C:\...…` so snapshots remain stable across operating systems.

**Data flow**: It accepts any `Into<String>`, converts to a mutable `String`, replaces full platform-rendered versions of `/tmp/project` and `/tmp/hooks.json` with those Unix literals, and if the platform path differs from `/tmp/project`, iterates reversed truncated prefixes from `truncated_path_variants` to replace `prefix…` with the matching Unix prefix plus ellipsis. It returns the normalized text.

**Call relations**: This is a lower-level snapshot utility used directly by tests and by `normalized_backend_snapshot` to sanitize backend-rendered multiline output.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (truncated_path_variants); 3 external calls (into, replace, format!).


##### `normalized_backend_snapshot`  (lines 65–89)

```
fn normalized_backend_snapshot(value: &T) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Normalizes backend-rendered display output for snapshot assertions while preserving quoted-line alignment. It handles both ordinary lines and lines wrapped in quotes with width-sensitive formatting.

**Data flow**: It formats the input displayable value into a string, short-circuits on Unix-like test paths, otherwise processes each line: quoted lines are unwrapped, path-normalized via `normalize_snapshot_paths`, then re-quoted with the original character width; unquoted lines are normalized directly. It joins the transformed lines with newlines and returns the result.

**Call relations**: This helper sits on top of `normalize_snapshot_paths` for tests that snapshot backend display output rather than raw widget rendering.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (format!).


##### `invalid_value`  (lines 91–101)

```
fn invalid_value(
    candidate: impl Into<String>,
    allowed: impl Into<String>,
) -> ConstraintError
```

**Purpose**: Constructs a `ConstraintError::InvalidValue` with placeholder metadata for tests. It saves tests from spelling out the full enum payload when only candidate and allowed values matter.

**Data flow**: It takes `candidate` and `allowed` strings, converts them into owned `String`s, fills fixed values for `field_name` and `requirement_source`, and returns the resulting `ConstraintError` enum variant.

**Call relations**: This is an isolated fixture constructor used by tests that need to compare validation errors.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (into).


##### `snapshot`  (lines 103–118)

```
fn snapshot(percent: f64) -> RateLimitSnapshot
```

**Purpose**: Builds a minimal `RateLimitSnapshot` fixture with a single primary window. Tests use it to simulate rate-limit percentages without populating unrelated fields.

**Data flow**: It reads a `f64` percentage, rounds it to `i32`, inserts it into a `RateLimitWindow` with a 60-minute duration and no reset time, wraps that as `primary`, leaves all other snapshot fields as `None`, and returns the `RateLimitSnapshot`.

**Call relations**: This is a standalone fixture helper for tests that exercise rate-limit UI or status formatting.


##### `test_session_telemetry`  (lines 120–135)

```
fn test_session_telemetry(config: &Config, model: &str) -> SessionTelemetry
```

**Purpose**: Creates a `SessionTelemetry` instance suitable for tests, using offline model metadata and fixed origin/session labels. It avoids any dependency on live model lookup or account identity.

**Data flow**: It reads the provided `Config` and model slug, derives model info with `construct_model_info_offline_for_tests(config.to_models_manager_config())`, then passes a fresh `ThreadId`, the model identifiers, several `None` account/auth fields, fixed strings like `test_originator` and `test`, and the CLI session source into `SessionTelemetry::new`. It returns the telemetry object.

**Call relations**: This helper is called by `make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth` during widget construction so tests get a realistic telemetry object without external services.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (new, new, session_source_cli); called by 1 (make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth); 1 external calls (to_models_manager_config).

)
```

**Purpose**: Creates a manually driven `ChatWidget` test instance with default unauthenticated account flags. It returns the widget plus the raw `AppEvent` and `Op` receivers so tests can inspect emitted side effects.

**Data flow**: It forwards the optional model override and fixed `false` auth booleans into `make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth`, awaits the result, and returns the tuple unchanged.

**Call relations**: This is the common entry helper used by tests such as hook snapshots, queued-message editing assertions, and the sender-returning wrapper.

##### `make_chatwidget_manual_with_auth`  (lines 159–212)

    ChatWidget,
    tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedRec
```

**Purpose**: Builds a fully initialized `ChatWidget` wired to test channels and seeded with deterministic UI state. It is the core fixture constructor for ChatWidget tests, including optional model override and simulated auth capabilities.

**Data flow**: It creates unbounded channels for `AppEvent` and `Op`, wraps the app-event sender, loads `Config` via `test_config`, resolves the effective model from the override or offline default, optionally writes `cfg.model`, creates `SessionTelemetry` and a test `ModelCatalog`, assembles a `ChatWidgetInit` with dummy frame requester and fixed booleans/placeholder fields, constructs the widget with direct op targeting, then mutates transcript activity, placeholder strings, bottom-pane placeholder text, and selected model. It returns `(ChatWidget, AppEvent receiver, Op receiver)`.

**Call relations**: This is the underlying constructor called by `make_chatwidget_manual`; many higher-level tests depend on it indirectly to obtain a widget in a known state.

*Call graph*: calls 6 internal fn (new, new, test_config, test_model_catalog, test_session_telemetry, test_dummy); called by 1 (make_chatwidget_manual); 4 external calls (new, new, new_with_op_target, Direct).


##### `next_submit_op`  (lines 216–225)

```
fn next_submit_op(op_rx: &mut tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<Op>) -> Op
```

**Purpose**: Pulls from an `Op` receiver until it finds the next `Op::UserTurn`. It ignores unrelated ops and fails loudly if the queue is empty or disconnected before a submission appears.

**Data flow**: It repeatedly calls `try_recv` on the mutable receiver, returns the first `Op::UserTurn { .. }`, skips any other `Op`, and panics on `Empty` or `Disconnected` errors.

**Call relations**: This helper is used by tests that submit input through the widget and need to assert the exact outbound user-turn operation despite other background ops sharing the same channel.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (try_recv, panic!).


##### `next_interrupt_op`  (lines 227–236)

```
fn next_interrupt_op(op_rx: &mut tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<Op>)
```

**Purpose**: Consumes an `Op` receiver until an `Op::Interrupt` is observed. It is the interrupt-specific counterpart to `next_submit_op`.

**Data flow**: It loops over `try_recv`, returns once it sees `Op::Interrupt { .. }`, discards unrelated ops, and panics if the channel is empty or closed before an interrupt arrives.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that trigger cancellation/interruption and need to verify the widget emitted the interrupt op among other channel traffic.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (try_recv, panic!).


##### `assert_no_submit_op`  (lines 238–245)

```
fn assert_no_submit_op(op_rx: &mut tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<Op>)
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that no queued `Op::UserTurn` exists on the op channel. It drains all currently available ops and asserts that none are submission operations.

**Data flow**: It repeatedly `try_recv`s until the receiver is empty, checking each op with `matches!`; if any `Op::UserTurn` is found it triggers an assertion failure including the unexpected op debug output.

**Call relations**: This is a negative assertion helper for tests that expect UI actions not to submit a user turn.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (try_recv, assert!).

```
fn drain_insert_history(
    rx: &mut tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<AppEvent>,
) -> Vec<Vec<ratatui::text::Line<'static>>>
```

**Purpose**: Drains pending `AppEvent`s and extracts only `InsertHistoryCell` payloads as rendered line groups. It also inserts blank separators between non-continuation cells to match transcript display expectations.

**Data flow**: It repeatedly `try_recv`s from the event receiver, filters for `AppEvent::InsertHistoryCell(cell)`, renders each cell with width 80, conditionally prepends an empty line when the cell is not a stream continuation and prior output exists, pushes the lines into an output vector, and returns `Vec<Vec<Line<'static>>>`.

**Call relations**: Used by `assert_hook_events_snapshot` to verify that hook completion writes history cells while hook start only updates the live hook cell.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (assert_hook_events_snapshot); 2 external calls (try_recv, new).

**Data flow**: It iterates over each line and each span within the line, appends `span.content` to a mutable `String`, appends `\n` after every line, and returns the accumulated string.

##### `begin_exec_with_source`  (lines 808–835)

```
fn begin_exec_with_source(
    chat: &mut ChatWidget,
    call_id: &str,
    raw_cmd: &str,
    source: ExecCommandSource,
) -> AppServerThreadItem
```

**Purpose**: Starts a synthetic command-execution item with a chosen `ExecCommandSource`, parsing the shell command into protocol command actions. It returns the exact started item so tests can later complete it consistently.

**Data flow**: It builds a `bash -lc <raw_cmd>` command vector, parses it with `codex_shell_command::parse_command::parse_command`, converts parsed actions into `AppServerCommandAction` values using the widget cwd, constructs `AppServerThreadItem::CommandExecution` with in-progress status and no output/exit code, sends it via `handle_exec_begin`, and returns the item clone.

**Call relations**: This is the core exec-start helper; `begin_exec` delegates to it for normal agent commands, while tests can call it directly to vary the command source.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (parse_command, shlex_join, handle_exec_begin); called by 1 (begin_exec); 1 external calls (vec!).

) -> AppServerThreadItem
```

**Purpose**: Starts a synthetic command-execution item specifically for `UnifiedExecStartup`. Unlike the generic exec helper, it sets a process ID immediately and leaves command actions empty.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need to distinguish startup execs from ordinary agent-issued commands.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (shlex_join, handle_exec_begin); 2 external calls (new, vec!).

fn end_exec(

```

**Purpose**: Completes a previously started command-execution item with aggregated output, exit code, and derived final status. It validates that the supplied begin item is actually a command execution.

**Purpose**: End-to-end asserts that the terminal-specific Shift+Left binding edits the newest queued message instead of the oldest. It sets up a running-task state, seeds two queued messages, triggers the key event, and checks both composer contents and queue mutation.

**Data flow**: It creates a widget via `make_chatwidget_manual`, computes and installs the queued-message edit binding for the supplied `TerminalInfo`, marks the bottom pane as task-running, pushes two `UserMessage`s into `chat.input_queue.queued_user_messages`, refreshes the pending-input preview, sends a `KeyEvent` for Shift+Left into `chat.handle_key_event`, then asserts that the composer text is `second queued` and the remaining queue contains only `first queued`.

**Call relations**: This is a higher-level assertion helper used directly by tests for terminal keybinding behavior; it depends on the standard widget fixture constructor.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (make_chatwidget_manual, from); 2 external calls (new, assert_eq!).

##### `strip_osc8_for_snapshot`  (lines 1246–1278)

```
fn strip_osc8_for_snapshot(text: &str) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Removes OSC 8 terminal hyperlink escape sequences from rendered text while preserving visible characters. This keeps snapshots focused on what users see rather than terminal hyperlink metadata.

**Data flow**: It scans the input string as bytes, detects OSC 8 sequences beginning with `ESC ] 8 ;;`, skips forward until either BEL or `ESC \` terminates the sequence, otherwise decodes and appends the next Unicode character to an output string, and returns the stripped result.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that snapshot rendered popup text containing terminal hyperlinks.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (with_capacity).

It returns the assembled summary.

async fn assert_hook_events_snapshot(

)
```

**Purpose**: Runs an end-to-end hook lifecycle assertion: start a hook, verify it updates only the live hook cell, complete it with output entries, drain inserted history cells, and snapshot the combined rendered history. It encodes several subtle expectations about hook UI behavior in one reusable helper.

**Data flow**: It creates a widget and event receiver via `make_chatwidget_manual`, sends a running hook notification using `handle_hook_started(hook_run(...Running...))`, asserts `drain_insert_history` is empty, forces visibility with `reveal_running_hooks`, asserts the active hook blob contains the expected `Running <event> hook: <status_message>` text, then sends a completed hook notification using `handle_hook_completed(hook_run(...Completed..., entries))`, drains inserted history cells, flattens them with `lines_to_single_string`, concatenates the results, and snapshots the combined string with `assert_chatwidget_snapshot!`.

**Call relations**: This is a high-level reusable assertion helper built on top of `make_chatwidget_manual`, `hook_run`, `handle_hook_started`, `handle_hook_completed`, `drain_insert_history`, and `reveal_running_hooks`.

*Call graph*: calls 6 internal fn (drain_insert_history, handle_hook_completed, handle_hook_started, hook_run, make_chatwidget_manual, reveal_running_hooks); 4 external calls (new, assert!, assert_chatwidget_snapshot!, vec!).

**Purpose**: Maps each `HookEventName` enum variant to the exact label string expected in rendered hook text. It keeps hook snapshot assertions aligned with UI wording.

**Data flow**: It pattern-matches the supplied `HookEventName` and returns a static string such as `PreToolUse`, `PermissionRequest`, `SessionStart`, or `Stop`.

**Call relations**: Used by `assert_hook_events_snapshot` when checking the live hook-cell text after a hook starts.


### `tui/tests/all.rs`

`test` · `test startup`

This file is the root of the TUI integration test target. Rather than keeping many standalone integration test crates under `tests/`, it builds one aggregate test binary and includes the actual test suites as Rust modules. The top-level `mod test_backend;` exposes a test-only backend shim used by the suite modules, and `mod suite;` recursively includes the concrete test cases collected under `tests/suite/`.

A notable detail is the `#![allow(clippy::expect_used)]` crate attribute, which relaxes linting for test code where explicit `expect` calls are acceptable and often desirable for failure diagnostics. The `use codex_cli as _;` import is intentionally unused at the source level but forces Cargo to retain the `codex_cli` dev-dependency; the comment explains that the tests spawn the `codex` binary, so this import prevents dependency-pruning tools such as cargo-shear from incorrectly removing it. There is no runtime logic here: its entire role is compile-time composition of the integration test harness and its supporting modules.


### `tui/tests/suite/mod.rs`

`test` · `test discovery/compilation`

This module is a pure test-suite aggregator. Its only job is to declare the concrete integration test modules that used to exist as separate standalone tests and now live under `tests/suite/`. By listing `resize_reflow`, `status_indicator`, `vt100_history`, and `vt100_live_commit`, it causes those files to be compiled as submodules of the single `tui/tests/all.rs` integration test crate.

That organization matters because Rust normally treats each file in `tests/` as a separate crate, which can increase compile overhead and fragment shared helpers. Consolidating them under this module allows the test backend and any shared fixtures to be reused through normal module visibility instead of duplicated across binaries. There is no executable control flow or state in this file; its effect is entirely structural at compile time. The module names also document the major behavioral areas covered by the TUI integration tests: terminal resize/reflow behavior, status indicator rendering, VT100 history handling, and live commit behavior in the VT100 path.


### `tui/tests/test_backend.rs`

`test` · `test setup/compilation`

This file is a thin adapter module for integration tests. The `#[path = "../src/test_backend.rs"]` attribute tells Rust to compile the backend implementation from the crate's source tree as a local module named `inner`, even though it physically lives outside the `tests/` directory. The file then publicly re-exports `inner::VT100Backend` so the integration tests can refer to that backend type directly through the test crate's module structure.

The design choice here is important: integration tests are separate crates and cannot automatically access private crate internals. By explicitly including the source file with `#[path]`, the test harness gets a copy of the backend implementation without needing to make it part of the public API of the main crate. This keeps production visibility tighter while still enabling realistic terminal-behavior tests. There is no runtime logic in this wrapper; its value is in compile-time inclusion and namespace shaping for the test suites that need a VT100-compatible backend abstraction.


### App orchestration and support tests
This group covers top-level App behavior, startup and session helpers, transcript rendering, and a handful of focused TUI support regressions outside the chat widget.

### `tui/src/app/test_support.rs`

`test` · `test setup and assertions`

This small support module centralizes heavyweight `App` construction for tests so sibling app test modules do not each duplicate the same initialization boilerplate. `make_test_app` builds a `ChatWidget` plus event sender using `make_chatwidget_manual_with_sender`, clones its config, creates a `FileSearchManager`, resolves an offline model slug, and constructs `SessionTelemetry`. It then fills out a full `App` struct with test-safe defaults: no workspace command runner or state DB, default loader/cloud overrides, empty transcript and thread state, default `TranscriptReflowState`, default `SkillLoadWarningState` and `BacktrackState`, embedded app-server target, default environment manager for tests, and empty maps/queues for thread listeners and pending writes. The resulting fixture is close to a real app instance, which makes behavior tests more representative.

`test_session_telemetry` is a focused helper that derives offline model info from the config’s models-manager settings and creates a `SessionTelemetry` with synthetic thread id, originator, and session source values suitable for tests.

`app_enabled_in_effective_config` is a narrow config-inspection utility for assertions. It walks the effective TOML config table down through `apps.<app_id>.enabled` and returns `Option<bool>`, allowing tests to verify whether an app-specific enable flag is present and what value won after config layering.

#### Function details

##### `make_test_app`  (lines 12–70)

```
async fn make_test_app() -> App
```

**Purpose**: Constructs a fully initialized `App` fixture with realistic defaults and offline model metadata for unit tests. It mirrors production structure closely while avoiding external dependencies.

**Data flow**: Awaits `make_chatwidget_manual_with_sender()` to get a `ChatWidget` and `AppEventSender`, clones the widget config, creates `FileSearchManager::new(config.cwd.to_path_buf(), app_event_tx.clone())`, resolves the current model via `get_model_offline_for_tests`, builds telemetry with `test_session_telemetry`, and returns an `App` literal populated with default/empty state for transcript, overlays, thread channels, navigation, pending requests, and config overrides.

**Call relations**: Used broadly across app tests as the standard fixture constructor. It delegates model/telemetry setup to `test_session_telemetry` and leaves channels internal to the widget fixture.

*Call graph*: calls 8 internal fn (default, without_managed_config_for_tests, default_for_tests, new, get_model_offline_for_tests, test_session_telemetry, new, defaults); called by 18 (mcp_inventory_omits_thread_id_for_closed_agent_thread, overridden_disabled_guardian_does_not_apply_auto_review_companions, rebuild_config_for_resume_or_fallback_errors_when_cwd_changes, rebuild_config_for_resume_or_fallback_uses_current_config_on_same_cwd_error, refresh_in_memory_config_from_disk_best_effort_keeps_current_config_on_error, refresh_in_memory_config_from_disk_keeps_cloud_requirements_for_thread_transitions, refresh_in_memory_config_from_disk_loads_latest_apps_state, refresh_in_memory_config_from_disk_updates_resize_reflow_config, refresh_in_memory_config_from_disk_uses_active_chat_widget_cwd, sync_tui_pet_disabled_updates_chat_widget_config_copy (+8 more)); 13 external calls (new, new, new, new, new, default, make_chatwidget_manual_with_sender, default, default, default (+3 more)).


##### `test_session_telemetry`  (lines 72–88)

```
fn test_session_telemetry(config: &Config, model: &str) -> SessionTelemetry
```

**Purpose**: Builds deterministic `SessionTelemetry` for tests using offline model metadata and synthetic identity/session-source values. This avoids networked model catalog dependencies.

**Data flow**: Takes `config` and `model`, derives `model_info` with `construct_model_info_offline_for_tests(model, &config.to_models_manager_config())`, then returns `SessionTelemetry::new(...)` with a fresh `ThreadId`, the model slug, fixed originator strings, disabled prompt logging, and a deserialized `"cli"` session source.

**Call relations**: Called by `make_test_app` to populate the `App.session_telemetry` field in a realistic but deterministic way.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (construct_model_info_offline_for_tests, new, new); called by 1 (make_test_app); 3 external calls (to_models_manager_config, from_value, json!).


##### `app_enabled_in_effective_config`  (lines 90–101)

```
fn app_enabled_in_effective_config(config: &Config, app_id: &str) -> Option<bool>
```

**Purpose**: Reads the effective layered config and extracts `apps.<app_id>.enabled` if present. It is a convenience helper for tests asserting config-layer outcomes.

**Data flow**: Traverses `config.config_layer_stack.effective_config()` as nested TOML tables: root table → `apps` table → app-id table → `enabled` value, converting the final value with `TomlValue::as_bool`. Returns `Option<bool>`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need to inspect the merged effective config rather than raw config fields.


### `tui/src/app/tests.rs`

`test` · `test suite execution`

This is the main integration-style unit test file for the TUI app layer. It imports a large cross-section of app, chat widget, app-server protocol, and config types, then exercises behavior at the `App` boundary rather than isolated helper functions. The file is organized around realistic scenarios: replaying buffered thread events into a newly attached primary thread, switching among agent threads, handling inactive-thread approvals, side-conversation lifecycle, startup/shutdown flows, config persistence, rollback/backtrack, resize reflow row caps, and thread settings synchronization.

A substantial portion of the file is test scaffolding. `make_test_app` and `make_test_app_with_channels` construct realistic `App` fixtures; `test_thread_session`, `test_turn`, and notification/request constructors synthesize app-server state; `lines_to_single_string`, `rendered_line_text`, and `next_user_turn_op` make assertions on rendered transcript output and queued ops concise. Snapshot helpers verify stable user-visible text for warnings, picker labels, clear-screen headers, and confirmation popups.

The tests emphasize invariants that span multiple subsystems: replayed snapshots must restore draft input and collaboration mode without prematurely submitting queued turns; inactive-thread approvals must preserve context and surface correctly; side threads must hide inherited fork history and defer parent overlays; thread switching must reseed collab metadata; resize reflow must retain only the transcript tail under row caps and respect pet-induced wrap width changes; and config-changing actions must update both in-memory state and persisted config files. Overall, this file documents the expected behavior of the app’s orchestration layer under many edge cases and race conditions.

#### Function details

##### `test_absolute_path`  (lines 119–121)

```
fn test_absolute_path(path: &str) -> AbsolutePathBuf
```

**Purpose**: Creates an `AbsolutePathBuf` from a string literal for tests, panicking if the input is not absolute. It keeps path-heavy assertions concise.

**Data flow**: Converts the input `&str` into `PathBuf`, passes it to `AbsolutePathBuf::try_from`, and unwraps with `expect("absolute test path")`.

**Call relations**: Used by tests that need absolute filesystem paths in approval and thread-settings payloads.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (try_from); called by 2 (inactive_thread_permissions_approval_preserves_file_system_permissions, inactive_thread_settings_notification_updates_cached_collaboration_mode); 1 external calls (from).


##### `next_thread_settings_updated`  (lines 123–144)

```
async fn next_thread_settings_updated(
    app_server: &mut AppServerSession,
    thread_id: ThreadId,
) -> ThreadSettingsUpdatedNotification
```

**Purpose**: Polls the app-server event stream until it finds a `ThreadSettingsUpdated` notification for a specific thread. It fails fast if the event does not arrive within a bounded number of attempts.

**Data flow**: Loops up to 20 times, each time awaiting `time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(2), app_server.next_event())`, unwrapping the event stream result, pattern-matching for `AppServerEvent::ServerNotification(ServerNotification::ThreadSettingsUpdated(notification))`, and returning the matching notification when `notification.thread_id == thread_id.to_string()`. If no match is found, it panics.

**Call relations**: Used by the thread-settings update test to observe the server-side notification emitted after submitting an override-turn-context operation.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (next_event); called by 1 (override_turn_context_sends_thread_settings_update); 4 external calls (panic!, to_string, from_secs, timeout).


##### `handle_mcp_inventory_result_respects_origin_thread`  (lines 147–182)

async fn handle_mcp_inventory_result_respects_origin_thread()
```

**Purpose**: Tests that MCP inventory results only clear the loading cell when they belong to the active or app-scoped context, not an unrelated thread. This protects transcript state from cross-thread contamination.

**Data flow**: Builds a test app, pushes an MCP inventory loading cell into `transcript_cells`, calls `handle_mcp_inventory_result` once with `thread_id: None` and once with a mismatched thread id while `active_thread_id` is set, then asserts transcript length changes only in the first case.

**Call relations**: Exercises app logic that routes MCP inventory completion to the correct transcript context.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (new, make_test_app); 5 external calls (new, new, assert_eq!, new_mcp_inventory_loading, vec!).

##### `enqueue_primary_thread_session_replays_buffered_approval_after_attach`  (lines 197–248)

async fn enqueue_primary_thread_session_replays_buffered_approval_after_attach() -> Result<()>
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that an approval request buffered before the primary thread session is attached is replayed into the active receiver and becomes actionable after attachment. It also checks that approving it submits a thread-scoped operation.

**Data flow**: Creates an app with channels, synthesizes an approval request, records it in `pending_app_server_requests`, enqueues it before `enqueue_primary_thread_session`, then reads the replayed `ThreadBufferedEvent` from `active_thread_rx`, feeds it through `handle_thread_event_now`, simulates pressing `y`, and scans emitted `AppEvent`s for `SubmitThreadOp` targeting the attached thread.

**Call relations**: Tests the interaction between pending request buffering, primary-thread attachment, replay, and approval submission.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (new, exec_approval_request, make_test_app_with_channels, test_thread_session); 8 external calls (from_millis, Char, new, new, assert!, assert_eq!, panic!, timeout).


##### `resolved_buffered_approval_does_not_become_actionable_after_drain`  (lines 251–308)

```
async fn resolved_buffered_approval_does_not_become_actionable_after_drain() -> Result<()>
```

**Purpose**: Ensures a buffered approval request that was already resolved before replay does not become actionable when later drained into the active thread. This prevents stale approvals from resurfacing.

**Data flow**: Attaches a primary thread, enqueues an approval request, resolves it through `pending_app_server_requests`, dismisses it in the widget, drains app events, then reads the buffered request from `active_thread_rx`, handles it, simulates approval input, and asserts no `SubmitThreadOp` is emitted.

**Call relations**: Covers a race between request buffering and resolution in the replay path.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (new, exec_approval_request, make_test_app_with_channels, test_thread_session); 8 external calls (Integer, from_millis, Char, new, new, assert!, assert_eq!, timeout).

**Purpose**: Checks that replayed historical turns are emitted before an initial queued prompt is auto-submitted when attaching a primary thread. This preserves transcript ordering and avoids sending the new prompt before old history is visible.

**Data flow**: Builds a custom `ChatWidget` with an initial user message, attaches a primary thread session containing one completed historical turn, drains emitted `AppEvent`s, records whether replayed history cells mention the earlier prompt, and captures the submitted `Op::UserTurn` items. It asserts replay happened and the submitted items match the initial prompt.

**Call relations**: Tests startup/session-attach ordering between replay and initial prompt submission.

*Call graph*: calls 7 internal fn (new, get_model_offline_for_tests, new, lines_to_single_string, make_test_app_with_channels, test_thread_session, test_dummy); 6 external calls (new, assert!, assert_eq!, create_initial_user_message, new_with_app_event, vec!).

##### `enqueue_thread_event_does_not_block_when_channel_full`  (lines 460–496)

```

**Purpose**: Checks that enqueuing thread notifications remains non-blocking even when the per-thread channel is full. This protects the app from deadlocking on slow consumers.

**Data flow**: Creates a thread channel with capacity 1, marks it active, enqueues one `ThreadClosed` notification, then wraps a second enqueue in a short timeout to ensure it completes. It then drains two events from the receiver to confirm both were accepted.

**Call relations**: Exercises the thread-event buffering strategy under backpressure.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (new, make_test_app, thread_closed_notification, new); 2 external calls (from_millis, timeout).

async fn replay_only_thread_keeps_restored_queue_visible()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that replay-only threads restore queued input visibly but do not auto-submit it. Replay-only threads cannot safely resume live execution.

**Data flow**: Constructs queued input state, replays a snapshot with a completed turn but passes `resume_restored_queue = false`, then asserts the queued message remains visible in the widget and no user-turn op is emitted.

**Call relations**: Covers the replay-only branch of snapshot replay queue restoration.

```
async fn replay_thread_snapshot_keeps_queue_when_running_state_only_comes_from_snapshot()
```

##### `next_user_turn_op`  (lines 4808–4817)

```
fn next_user_turn_op(op_rx: &mut tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<Op>) -> Op
```

**Purpose**: Drains an op receiver until it finds the next `Op::UserTurn`, panicking with a summary of seen ops if none is present. It simplifies tests that expect exactly one user-turn submission.

**Data flow**: Repeatedly calls `try_recv()` on the provided receiver, returning the first op matching `Op::UserTurn { .. }`. Non-matching ops are formatted into a `seen` vector. If the receiver empties first, it panics with the collected debug strings.

**Call relations**: Used by several replay/input-restoration tests to assert the next submitted user turn.

### `tui/src/app/tests/model_catalog.rs`

`test` · `test suite execution`

This test module focuses on the startup/model-catalog helpers defined in `startup_prompts.rs`. It uses the shared test model preset list (`crate::test_support::TEST_MODEL_PRESETS`) as realistic catalog input and then mutates copies of those presets to exercise edge cases around migration prompts and availability tooltips.

The helper functions keep assertions readable. `all_model_presets` clones the shared preset list, `model_availability_nux_config` builds a `ModelAvailabilityNuxConfig` from `(model, count)` pairs, and `model_migration_copy_to_plain_text` flattens rich migration prompt copy into plain text for snapshot assertions.

The tests cover three main behaviors. First, migration prompt eligibility: prompts should appear only for deprecated/upgradable models, should be suppressed when the target equals the current model or the migration was already acknowledged, and should not appear when the target preset is missing or hidden from the picker. A dedicated snapshot test confirms that hidden current models can still produce migration prompt copy when they upgrade to a visible target. Second, model-availability NUX selection: the first eligible preset in catalog order wins, exhausted models are skipped once their shown count reaches `MODEL_AVAILABILITY_NUX_MAX_SHOW_COUNT`, and `None` is returned when all candidates are exhausted. Third, persistence side effects: `prepare_startup_tooltip_override` must increment and persist shown counts, and `apply_accepted_model_migration` must update config plus emit the expected sequence of app events for acknowledgement, model update, reasoning-effort update, and persisted selection.

#### Function details

##### `all_model_presets`  (lines 8–10)

```
fn all_model_presets() -> Vec<ModelPreset>
```

**Purpose**: Returns a cloned copy of the shared test model preset catalog. Tests mutate the returned vector freely without affecting global fixtures.

**Data flow**: Clones `crate::test_support::TEST_MODEL_PRESETS` and returns the new `Vec<ModelPreset>`.

**Call relations**: Used by nearly every test in this module as the baseline model catalog input.

*Call graph*: called by 7 (model_migration_prompt_shows_for_hidden_model, model_migration_prompt_skips_when_target_missing_or_hidden, prepare_startup_tooltip_override_persists_model_availability_nux_count, select_model_availability_nux_picks_only_eligible_model, select_model_availability_nux_returns_none_when_all_models_are_exhausted, select_model_availability_nux_skips_missing_and_exhausted_models, select_model_availability_nux_uses_existing_model_order_as_priority).

### `tui/src/app/tests/session_summary.rs`

`test` · `test suite execution`

This compact test module exercises the `session_summary` helper’s decision logic and output formatting. The helper is used when starting a fresh session or resuming another one so the app can append a short summary of the previous conversation, including token usage and a command hint for resuming it later.

The tests focus on the conditions under which a summary should or should not exist. If there is no token usage and no valid resume hint, `session_summary` should return `None`. A resume hint also depends on the rollout path actually existing on disk; merely having a thread id and a nominal rollout path is not enough. When a persisted rollout file exists, the helper should produce both a usage line and a `codex resume <thread-id>` hint. If the thread also has a human-readable name, the resume hint changes shape to instruct the user to resume first and then select the named session in the picker, including both the name and thread id.

Together these tests document that `session_summary` is intentionally conservative about offering resume guidance: it only does so when there is concrete persisted rollout state to resume from, and it adapts the hint text based on whether the thread would be identified by id alone or by a named picker entry.

#### Function details

##### `session_summary_skips_when_no_usage_or_resume_hint`  (lines 5–15)

```
async fn session_summary_skips_when_no_usage_or_resume_hint()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that `session_summary` returns `None` when there is neither token usage nor a resumable session hint. Empty summaries should not produce transcript noise.

**Data flow**: Calls `session_summary` with `TokenUsage::default()`, no thread id, no thread name, and no rollout path, then asserts the result is `None`.

**Call relations**: Tests the base no-summary branch of the session-summary helper.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (assert!).


##### `session_summary_skips_resume_hint_until_rollout_exists`  (lines 18–33)

```

```

**Purpose**: Checks that a thread id alone is not enough to produce a resume hint; the rollout file must actually exist. This prevents misleading resume guidance.

**Data flow**: Creates default token usage, a parsed `ThreadId`, and a temp rollout path that is not written to disk, calls `session_summary`, and asserts the result is `None`.

**Call relations**: Tests the persisted-rollout existence requirement for resume hints.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_string); 2 external calls (assert!, default).

```

**Purpose**: Verifies that when token usage is nonzero and the rollout file exists, `session_summary` returns both a usage line and a direct `codex resume <thread-id>` hint. This is the standard resumable-session case.

**Data flow**: Builds nonzero `TokenUsage`, a `ThreadId`, creates and writes a temp rollout file, calls `session_summary`, unwraps the result, and asserts the `usage_line` and `resume_hint` strings match the expected formatting.

**Call relations**: Tests the positive resumable-session branch of the helper.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_string); 3 external calls (default, assert_eq!, write).

**Call relations**: Tests the named-thread formatting branch of the session-summary helper.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_string); 3 external calls (default, assert_eq!, write).


### `tui/src/app/tests/startup.rs`

`test` · `startup and resume-path verification`

This test file focuses on the narrow startup window where the app has not yet fully attached a primary thread. Several unit tests validate the pure gating helpers on `App`: fresh starts and explicit exit selections must wait for initial session configuration, while resume/fork selections bypass that wait; paused-goal prompting is only enabled for a quiet resume with no initial prompt text and no startup images; and active-thread event draining remains disabled until a primary thread id exists. The async tests then move beyond pure predicates into startup attachment behavior. They construct a test app with channels, queue composer input before startup completes, and verify that `handle_startup_thread_started` flushes that queued text as a `Op::UserTurn` once a fresh session attaches. Failure handling is also checked: startup thread creation errors must clear the pending-start flag, leave `primary_thread_id` unset, and return a descriptive error instead of leaving chat state half-configured. Another test covers stale startup notifications by preloading routing state for both a primary and a stale thread, then asserting that attaching the stale startup thread removes its local channel and agent-navigation entry without disturbing the active primary thread. The final pair of tests define the UX contract for same-thread resume requests: resuming the currently active thread is ignored with an informational history cell, but reattaching a merely displayed inactive thread is allowed.

#### Function details

##### `startup_waiting_gate_is_only_for_fresh_or_exit_session_selection`  (lines 8–35)

```
fn startup_waiting_gate_is_only_for_fresh_or_exit_session_selection()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies the startup wait predicate for each `SessionSelection` variant. It establishes that only `StartFresh` and `Exit` keep the app in the initial-session waiting state.

**Data flow**: The test constructs concrete `SessionSelection` values, including `Resume` and `Fork` targets with `PathBuf` paths and fresh `ThreadId`s, passes references into `App::should_wait_for_initial_session`, and compares the returned booleans against expected values. It does not mutate shared state; its output is assertion success or failure.

**Call relations**: This is a standalone unit test invoked by the Rust test harness. It directly probes the helper used by startup orchestration so later routing logic can rely on the exact variant split.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (assert_eq!).


##### `startup_paused_goal_prompt_gate_is_only_for_quiet_resume`  (lines 38–71)

```
fn startup_paused_goal_prompt_gate_is_only_for_quiet_resume()
```

**Purpose**: Checks the predicate that decides whether a resumed thread should immediately prompt about a paused goal. The test encodes the intended UX: only a plain resume with no startup prompt and no startup images should trigger the prompt.

**Data flow**: It builds `Resume`, `Fork`, empty-image, and one-image inputs, then calls `App::should_prompt_for_paused_goal_after_startup_resume` with different combinations of session selection, optional initial prompt text, and image slices. The returned booleans are asserted without mutating app state.

**Call relations**: The test is run by the test harness and documents the guard conditions consumed by startup resume flow before `maybe_prompt_resume_paused_goal_after_resume` would be reached.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 6 external calls (from, new, assert!, Fork, Resume, vec!).


##### `startup_waiting_gate_holds_active_thread_events_until_primary_thread_configured`  (lines 74–106)

```
fn startup_waiting_gate_holds_active_thread_events_until_primary_thread_configured()
```

**Purpose**: Validates the two-step startup gate that suppresses active-thread event handling until a primary thread id has been configured. It demonstrates both the blocked and unblocked phases explicitly.

**Data flow**: The test derives an initial waiting flag from `StartFresh`, feeds that flag plus a synthetic receiver-present boolean into `App::should_handle_active_thread_events`, then checks `App::should_stop_waiting_for_initial_session` first with `None` and then with `Some(ThreadId::new())`. When the stop predicate becomes true, the local `wait_for_initial_session` variable is flipped to false and rechecked against the event-handling predicate.

**Call relations**: This test is a pure control-flow specification for startup orchestration. It is invoked by the test harness and captures how the startup loop should transition from buffering to normal event draining.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (assert_eq!, should_stop_waiting_for_initial_session, should_wait_for_initial_session).


##### `startup_waiting_gate_not_applied_for_resume_or_fork_session_selection`  (lines 109–136)

```

**Purpose**: Confirms that resumed or forked sessions do not inherit the fresh-session startup event gate. The app should process active-thread events immediately for those selections.

**Data flow**: It creates `Resume` and `Fork` selections, computes the waiting flag with `App::should_wait_for_initial_session`, and then passes that flag into `App::should_handle_active_thread_events` with `has_active_thread_receiver = true`. Assertions verify that both cases return true for event handling.

**Call relations**: The test is called by the test harness and complements the previous waiting-gate tests by covering the non-blocking startup branches.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 5 external calls (from, assert_eq!, should_wait_for_initial_session, Fork, Resume).

**Purpose**: Ensures that user input typed before a fresh startup session is fully configured is queued and then submitted once the startup thread attaches. This protects early composer input from being lost during startup.

**Data flow**: The test creates a channel-backed app, sets `pending_startup_thread_start = true`, enables queueing in `chat_widget`, injects external text, and simulates Enter to queue a submission. It then reads the next outbound op from `op_rx` and asserts that it is `Op::UserTurn` containing a single `UserInput::Text` with the queued text.

**Data flow**: The test builds a test app, marks `pending_startup_thread_start = true`, starts an embedded app server, and calls `handle_startup_thread_started` with `Err("boom".to_string())`. It expects an error result, inspects the error string for the wrapped startup message, and then asserts that `pending_startup_thread_start` is false and `primary_thread_id` remains `None`.

**Call relations**: Run by the Tokio test harness, this test covers the failure branch of startup attachment and documents that startup errors are terminal for that path rather than recoverable in-place.

*Call graph*: 4 external calls (pin, assert!, assert_eq!, start_embedded_app_server_for_picker).

### `tui/src/app/agent_status_feed_tests.rs`

`test` · `test execution`

This test file constructs small `ThreadEventStore` instances and feeds them concrete `ServerNotification::ItemCompleted` events to exercise the agent-status summarization logic end to end. The first test mixes a completed `ThreadItem::CommandExecution` from an agent with a huge `aggregated_output` payload and a follow-up `ThreadItem::AgentMessage`; it then builds an `AgentStatusThreadPreview` from the store, wraps it in an `AgentStatusHistoryCell`, renders display lines at width 80, and snapshot-checks that only the command line and concise agent message appear. The explicit negative assertion against `"unbounded output"` documents an important invariant: previews must use bounded buffered activity rather than raw accumulated process output.

The second test targets reasoning privacy. It inserts two completed `ThreadItem::Reasoning` items, one with a non-empty `summary` and one with only raw `content`. Rendering is expected to include only the safe summary text and omit both the hidden raw reasoning and summary-less reasoning content entirely. Together these tests pin down the preview-selection policy: command previews are concise, reasoning previews are summary-only, and unsafe or excessively large text must not leak into the agent-status UI.

#### Function details

##### `agent_status_uses_bounded_buffered_activity`  (lines 8–61)

```
fn agent_status_uses_bounded_buffered_activity()
```

**Purpose**: Builds a thread event history containing a large agent command completion plus an agent message, renders the agent-status cell, and verifies the preview stays concise. It specifically proves that massive `aggregated_output` text is excluded from the rendered status feed.

**Data flow**: Creates a `ThreadEventStore` with capacity 8, pushes two `ServerNotification::ItemCompleted` values containing a `ThreadItem::CommandExecution` and `ThreadItem::AgentMessage`, then derives an `AgentStatusThreadPreview` from the store for `/root/reviewer`. That preview is rendered through `AgentStatusHistoryCell::display_lines`, joined into a newline-delimited string, snapshot-compared against the expected compact output, and checked to ensure the repeated command output text never appears.

**Call relations**: This is a standalone `#[test]` entrypoint. Inside the test it drives the same preview-building path production code uses—store creation, preview extraction, history-cell construction, and line rendering—to validate the bounded-activity behavior at the UI boundary.

*Call graph*: calls 4 internal fn (new, from_store, new, try_from); 5 external calls (ItemCompleted, new, assert!, assert_snapshot!, vec!).


##### `agent_status_uses_reasoning_summaries_only`  (lines 64–109)

```
fn agent_status_uses_reasoning_summaries_only()
```

**Purpose**: Exercises reasoning-item rendering in the agent-status feed and confirms that only `summary` entries are surfaced. It also verifies that raw reasoning `content` is suppressed whether or not a summary exists.

**Data flow**: Creates a bounded `ThreadEventStore`, pushes two completed reasoning items for the same thread and turn—one with `summary = ["safe summary"]` and one with an empty summary but non-empty raw content—then builds a preview and renders it through `AgentStatusHistoryCell`. The rendered text is snapshot-asserted to contain only the safe summary, and explicit assertions confirm neither hidden raw reasoning string is present.

**Call relations**: This is another isolated `#[test]` that invokes the preview/render pipeline directly. It complements the buffered-activity test by covering the reasoning branch of preview selection and documenting the privacy rule enforced by `AgentStatusThreadPreview::from_store` and the history cell renderer.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (new, from_store, new); 5 external calls (ItemCompleted, new, assert!, assert_snapshot!, vec!).


### `tui/src/app/history_ui_tests.rs`

`test` · `test-time snapshot verification of transcript rendering`

This test file exercises the display contract of `HistoryCell` implementations by constructing concrete history events and converting their rendered lines into a newline-joined string for snapshot comparison. The two tests cover the desktop-thread lifecycle messages that appear in history: one for the normal "thread opened" informational event and one for the error variant produced when desktop thread launch fails. Rather than asserting on internal fields, the tests go through the same `display_lines(width)` path used by the UI, fixing the width at 80 columns so wrapping and formatting remain deterministic. The helper keeps the tests focused on semantic rendering by collecting each returned display line via `ToString` and joining them with `\n`, which matches how insta snapshots store multiline terminal content. The important design choice here is that the tests validate the final textual presentation, not just event construction, so regressions in formatting, prefixes, wrapping, or line ordering are caught even if the underlying event objects still exist.

#### Function details

##### `desktop_thread_opened_history_snapshot`  (lines 6–13)

```
fn desktop_thread_opened_history_snapshot()
```

**Purpose**: Builds the standard desktop-thread-opened info history cell and snapshots its rendered text. It ensures the user-visible transcript entry for a successful desktop thread open stays stable.

**Data flow**: Creates a history cell from `DESKTOP_THREAD_OPENED_MESSAGE.to_string()` with no hint via `history_cell::new_info_event`, passes the resulting cell through `render_cell`, and feeds the final multiline string into `insta::assert_snapshot!` under the `desktop_thread_opened_history` snapshot name. It does not mutate shared state.

**Call relations**: Invoked by the Rust test harness. It depends on the external history-cell constructor to produce the concrete event and on the snapshot assertion macro to compare the rendered output against the checked-in golden file.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (new_info_event, assert_snapshot!).


##### `desktop_thread_open_error_history_snapshot`  (lines 16–20)

```
fn desktop_thread_open_error_history_snapshot()
```

**Purpose**: Builds the desktop-thread-open-error history cell and snapshots its rendered text. It verifies the exact transcript wording and formatting shown when launching the desktop thread fails.

**Data flow**: Calls `desktop_thread_open_error_message("launch failed")` to obtain the message text, wraps it with `history_cell::new_error_event`, renders the cell to a newline-joined string through `render_cell`, and submits that string to `insta::assert_snapshot!` as `desktop_thread_open_error_history`. No persistent state is changed.

**Call relations**: Invoked by the test harness as the error-path companion to the success snapshot. It relies on the error-event constructor and snapshot framework to validate the final UI text.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (new_error_event, assert_snapshot!).


##### `render_cell`  (lines 22–29)

```
fn render_cell(cell: &impl HistoryCell) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Converts any `HistoryCell` into the exact multiline string used by the snapshot tests. It centralizes the width and line-joining logic so both tests compare the same rendering shape.

**Data flow**: Accepts a borrowed `impl HistoryCell`, calls `display_lines(80)` to obtain rendered line objects, maps each line through `ToString::to_string`, collects them into a `Vec<String>`, and joins them with newline separators into a single `String` return value. It reads only the cell's display output and writes no external state.

**Call relations**: Used internally by both snapshot tests to normalize rendering before assertion. Its only delegated behavior is the `HistoryCell::display_lines` call, which supplies the line-level presentation under test.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (display_lines).


### `tui/src/config_update_tests.rs`

`test` · `unit test execution`

This test file exercises three narrow invariants from `config_update.rs`. The first test verifies that `app_scoped_key_path` quotes dotted app IDs as JSON strings, ensuring an ID like `plugin.linear` becomes `apps."plugin.linear".enabled` rather than being split into nested path segments. The second test checks the exact `ConfigEdit` produced by `trusted_project_edit` for a representative project path, including the `projects."...".trust_level` key path, JSON `"trusted"` value, and `MergeStrategy::Replace`. The third test constructs a wrapped `color_eyre` error that mimics an app-server validation failure and asserts that `format_config_error` preserves both the outer TUI context and the inner server message in one flattened string.

These tests are intentionally small and concrete: they do not mock the app server or exercise async request code. Instead they pin down the string formatting and edit-shape details that higher-level config persistence depends on. Because these helpers feed protocol requests, regressions here would produce subtly wrong key paths or user-visible error messages even if the rest of the write pipeline remained intact.

#### Function details

##### `app_scoped_key_path_quotes_dotted_app_ids`  (lines 7–12)

```
fn app_scoped_key_path_quotes_dotted_app_ids()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that dotted app IDs are quoted when embedded into app-scoped config key paths. This prevents dots inside an app ID from being interpreted as path separators.

**Data flow**: The test calls `app_scoped_key_path("plugin.linear", "enabled")` and asserts that the returned string is exactly `apps."plugin.linear".enabled`.

**Call relations**: This test directly validates the quoting behavior implemented by `app_scoped_key_path` in the production module.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (assert_eq!).


##### `trusted_project_edit_targets_project_trust_level`  (lines 15–24)

```
fn trusted_project_edit_targets_project_trust_level()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies the exact `ConfigEdit` shape produced for trusting a project path. It checks both the key path and the replace semantics.

**Data flow**: The test calls `trusted_project_edit(Path::new("/workspace/team.project"))` and asserts equality with a manually constructed `ConfigEdit` containing the expected key path, JSON `"trusted"` value, and `MergeStrategy::Replace`.

**Call relations**: This test directly exercises `trusted_project_edit` and ensures its output matches what `write_trusted_project` will later send to the app server.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (assert_eq!).


##### `format_config_error_preserves_server_validation_message`  (lines 27–40)

```
fn format_config_error_preserves_server_validation_message()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that formatted config errors preserve nested app-server validation details rather than collapsing them into a generic outer message. It protects the user-visible diagnostics path.

**Data flow**: The test constructs a `color_eyre` error with an inner validation message, wraps it with `wrap_err("config/batchWrite failed in TUI")`, unwraps the error, passes it to `format_config_error`, and asserts the exact flattened string containing both contexts.

**Call relations**: This test validates the formatting contract of `format_config_error`, which higher-level config update flows use when surfacing write failures.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (assert_eq!, eyre!).
