### `tui/src/chatwidget/tests/status_surface_previews.rs`

`test` · `settings preview generation and setup popup rendering`

This file focuses on the preview machinery used by the status-line and terminal-title setup popups. It defines small helpers to convert `ratatui::text::Line` values into plain strings, ask the widget for preview data, render preview lines for selected `StatusLineItem` or `TerminalTitleItem` sets, and snapshot the setup popups themselves. Additional helpers seed cached project-root names and synthetic rate-limit snapshots so previews can exercise both live and fallback data paths.

The tests cover three main preview modes: fully live values (cached project root, branch, thread title, task progress), fully hardcoded fallback values when live data is absent, and mixed cases where some values are live and others come from preview defaults. Rate-limit preview tests verify that supported windows render into both status-line and terminal-title previews, while unavailable limit items are omitted cleanly. Setup-popup snapshots ensure the configuration UI itself shows the same preview logic for status line and terminal title. The file also captures two subtle behaviors: unnamed threads should fall back to the thread id in both surfaces, and missing project-root data intentionally uses different fallback strings for status line (`my-project`) versus terminal title (`project`). Finally, terminal-title preview tests verify that live thread and branch values are truncated using the same `ChatWidget::truncate_terminal_title_part` logic as the real title surface.

#### Function details

##### `line_text`  (lines 6–11)

```
fn line_text(line: Line<'static>) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Helper that converts a `ratatui::text::Line<'static>` into a plain owned `String` by concatenating span contents. It simplifies assertions on preview lines.

**Data flow**: It consumes the line’s spans, converts each span content into an owned string, concatenates them, and returns the resulting `String`.

**Call relations**: It is used by the terminal-title preview helper to normalize preview lines into plain text.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (title_preview_line).


##### `status_preview_line_option`  (lines 13–18)

```
fn status_preview_line_option(chat: &mut ChatWidget, items: &[StatusLineItem]) -> Option<String>
```

**Purpose**: Helper that asks the widget for status-surface preview data and renders an optional preview line for a chosen set of `StatusLineItem`s. It centralizes preview generation for status-line tests.

**Data flow**: It calls `chat.status_surface_preview_data()`, then `status_line_for_items(items.iter().copied(), true)` on the preview data, and maps the resulting line into text. It returns `Option<String>`.

**Call relations**: It is called by `status_preview_line` and directly by the unavailable-rate-limit preview test.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (status_preview_line); 2 external calls (iter, status_surface_preview_data).


##### `status_preview_line`  (lines 20–22)

```
fn status_preview_line(chat: &mut ChatWidget, items: &[StatusLineItem]) -> String
```

**Purpose**: Convenience wrapper around `status_preview_line_option` that expects a preview line to exist. It simplifies tests that know the selected items should render.

**Data flow**: It delegates to `status_preview_line_option`, unwraps the `Option`, and returns the preview string.

**Call relations**: It is used by tests that compare concrete status preview strings, such as missing-project-root behavior.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (status_preview_line_option); called by 1 (missing_project_root_uses_different_status_and_title_preview_sources).

**Data flow**: It calls `chat.terminal_title_preview_data()`, passes the selected items and preview data into `preview_line_for_title_items`, unwraps the resulting line, converts it with `line_text`, and returns the string.

**Call relations**: It is used by tests comparing terminal-title preview strings and truncation behavior.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (line_text); called by 2 (missing_project_root_uses_different_status_and_title_preview_sources, terminal_title_preview_uses_title_truncation_for_live_values); 2 external calls (preview_line_for_title_items, terminal_title_preview_data).

##### `cache_rate_limit_snapshot`  (lines 64–83)

```
fn cache_rate_limit_snapshot(chat: &mut ChatWidget)
```

**Purpose**: Helper that injects a synthetic rate-limit snapshot with monthly and weekly windows for preview tests. It prepares preview data for rate-limit items.

**Data flow**: It calls `chat.on_rate_limit_snapshot(Some(RateLimitSnapshot { ... }))` with a primary monthly window at 35% used and a secondary weekly window at 50% used.

### `tui/src/chatwidget/tests/terminal_title.rs`

`test` · `request handling`

This test file focuses narrowly on the chat widget’s terminal-title surface, especially the interaction between task-running state and execution approval prompts. Each test constructs a manual `ChatWidget` test harness with `make_chatwidget_manual`, marks the bottom pane as running, and then drives title recomputation through `refresh_terminal_title()` and `pre_draw_tick()`. To simulate approval-required state, the tests build concrete `ExecApprovalRequestEvent` values with realistic fields such as `call_id`, `approval_id`, `turn_id`, `command`, `cwd`, and `reason`, then inject them via `handle_exec_approval_request`.

The assertions distinguish two separate animation concepts: the normal activity spinner used while work is running, and the blinking action-required indicator used while approval is pending. The file verifies that pending approval suppresses the normal spinner and replaces the title with a `[ ! ] Action Required | project` or blinking `[ . ] ...` variant depending on elapsed animation time and configuration. It also checks that approving the request through a synthetic `'y'` keypress restores the ordinary project title and re-enables spinner animation. A final case disables `chat.config.animations` entirely to confirm that neither spinner nor blinking action-required indicators animate, while the static action-required warning still appears.

#### Function details

##### `terminal_title_shows_action_required_while_exec_approval_is_pending`  (lines 7–45)

```
async fn terminal_title_shows_action_required_while_exec_approval_is_pending()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies the full transition from normal running-task title, to pending-approval action-required title, back to normal activity title after approval is accepted. It specifically checks that approval-required state suppresses spinner animation until the approval is resolved.

**Data flow**: Creates a manual `ChatWidget`, sets `bottom_pane` task-running state to `true`, and injects an `ExecApprovalRequestEvent` rooted at the current working directory. After `pre_draw_tick()`, it reads `chat.last_terminal_title` and the title-animation predicates to confirm the warning title is active. It then sends a `'y'` key event into the widget, ticks again, and re-reads the title and spinner predicate to confirm the warning text is gone and normal spinner animation resumes.

**Call relations**: This is a top-level async test invoked by the test runner. It drives the same approval path the widget uses in production by calling the shared test helper `handle_exec_approval_request`, then exercises the widget’s key-event path to simulate user approval and observe the resulting title-state transition.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (current_dir); 5 external calls (Char, new, assert!, assert_eq!, vec!).


##### `terminal_title_action_required_respects_spinner_setting`  (lines 48–73)

```
async fn terminal_title_action_required_respects_spinner_setting()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that a custom terminal-title configuration can suppress the animated action-required title replacement. With the title explicitly configured to just `project`, the pending approval should not inject the warning prefix into the rendered title.

**Data flow**: Builds a manual widget, overwrites `chat.config.tui_terminal_title` with a single `project` segment, marks a task as running, and injects an exec approval request. After `pre_draw_tick()`, it reads `chat.last_terminal_title` and `should_animate_terminal_title_action_required()` to confirm the title remains exactly `project` and the action-required animation path is disabled.

**Call relations**: This test is run directly by the test harness. It follows the same setup path as the other terminal-title tests but varies configuration before the approval request arrives, proving that title rendering consults config state rather than always forcing the warning string.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (current_dir); 3 external calls (assert!, assert_eq!, vec!).


##### `terminal_title_action_required_blinks_when_animations_are_enabled`  (lines 76–104)

```
async fn terminal_title_action_required_blinks_when_animations_are_enabled()
```

**Purpose**: Confirms that when animations are enabled and enough time has elapsed, pending approval uses the blinking action-required variant rather than the static warning marker. The expected visible state here is the dotted `[ . ]` prefix.

**Data flow**: Creates a widget, marks work as running, manually backdates `terminal_title_animation_origin` by 1500 ms, refreshes the title baseline, and injects an approval request. After `pre_draw_tick()`, it reads `last_terminal_title` and the action-required animation predicate, expecting the blinking title string and a `true` animation flag.

**Call relations**: The test runner invokes this async test directly. It manipulates the widget’s internal animation clock before entering the approval state so the subsequent title recomputation takes the blinking branch instead of the initial static branch.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (current_dir); 5 external calls (now, assert!, assert_eq!, from_millis, vec!).


##### `terminal_title_activity_indicators_do_not_animate_when_animations_are_disabled`  (lines 107–139)

```
async fn terminal_title_activity_indicators_do_not_animate_when_animations_are_disabled()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies the global animation kill switch for terminal-title indicators. It checks both the ordinary running-task spinner and the approval-required blinking indicator under `config.animations = false`.

**Data flow**: Initializes a widget, sets `chat.config.animations` to `false`, marks a task as running, backdates the animation origin, and refreshes the title. It first reads the title and spinner predicate to confirm the running state stays at plain `project` with no spinner animation. It then injects an approval request, ticks, and reads the title plus action-required animation predicate to confirm a static `[ ! ] Action Required | project` title with no blinking.

**Call relations**: This test is another direct test-runner entry. It covers the same title recomputation paths as the earlier tests but forces the configuration branch that disables all animation-driven title changes.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (current_dir); 5 external calls (now, assert!, assert_eq!, from_millis, vec!).


### Rendering primitives and transcript formatting
These lower-level rendering tests validate markdown, history-cell formatting, layout allocation, status output, and token-activity visualizations used by the TUI.

### `tui/src/chatwidget/tokens/chart/palette_tests.rs`

`test` · `request handling`

This test file targets `TokenActivityPalette::from_parts` directly with controlled terminal and theme inputs. Rather than rendering full charts, it inspects the palette’s computed `Style` values and `uses_color` flag to ensure the palette-selection algorithm behaves correctly under dark backgrounds, light backgrounds, low-color terminals, non-RGB theme accents, and missing terminal color metadata.

The truecolor tests validate exact blended RGB values for empty cells, low-intensity active cells, full-intensity active cells, and bar styles. These assertions pin down the alpha-blending math and the distinction between dark and light background handling. The fallback-oriented tests verify that `Ansi16` terminals do not silently fall back to a generic green palette; instead they preserve the supplied theme accent style for active cells while using a dim default style for empty cells. Similar fallback expectations are checked when the theme accent is non-RGB or when terminal foreground/background RGB data is incomplete.

Together these tests document an important design choice: the palette prefers theme-consistent accent styling over synthetic gradients whenever the environment cannot support reliable blended colors. They also ensure that bold modifiers on the active style survive fallback construction.

#### Function details

##### `truecolor_palette_blends_theme_accent_against_dark_background`  (lines 7–35)

```
fn truecolor_palette_blends_theme_accent_against_dark_background()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies exact blended palette colors for a dark-background truecolor terminal. It checks empty-cell blending, low/high activity levels, bar style, and that the palette reports color-gradient mode.

**Data flow**: Constructs explicit dark terminal foreground/background RGB tuples and a bold RGB active style, passes them to `TokenActivityPalette::from_parts`, then reads `for_level(0)`, `for_level(1)`, `for_level(4)`, `for_bar_level(4)`, and `uses_color` to compare against expected RGB colors and `true`.

**Call relations**: Run directly by the unit-test harness. It exercises the blended-color branch of `from_parts` with deterministic inputs.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (from_parts, rgb_color); 3 external calls (default, assert!, assert_eq!).


##### `truecolor_palette_blends_empty_cell_for_light_background`  (lines 38–58)

```
fn truecolor_palette_blends_empty_cell_for_light_background()
```

**Purpose**: Checks the light-background branch of truecolor blending, especially the different empty-cell alpha used on light terminals. It confirms the palette still reaches full accent color at the highest level.

**Data flow**: Creates explicit light terminal colors and a bold RGB active style, builds a palette with `from_parts`, then reads `for_level(0)`, `for_level(4)`, and `uses_color` to assert the expected blended empty-cell RGB, full-intensity accent RGB, and `true` color mode.

**Call relations**: Invoked directly by the test runner. It complements the dark-background test by covering the alternate alpha-selection branch in `from_parts`.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (from_parts, rgb_color); 3 external calls (default, assert!, assert_eq!).


##### `ansi16_palette_uses_theme_accent_without_green_fallback`  (lines 61–76)

```
fn ansi16_palette_uses_theme_accent_without_green_fallback()
```

**Purpose**: Ensures low-color `Ansi16` terminals use the fallback palette while preserving the supplied theme accent style for active cells. This guards against an unintended generic-green fallback.

**Data flow**: Supplies terminal RGB values, `StdoutColorLevel::Ansi16`, and a bold magenta active style to `from_parts`, then reads `for_level(0)`, `for_level(1)`, `for_bar_level(4)`, and `uses_color` to confirm dim empty cells, accent-colored active/bar cells, and `false` color-gradient mode.

**Call relations**: Run directly by the unit-test harness. It covers the explicit low-color early-return branch in `from_parts`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_parts); 3 external calls (default, assert!, assert_eq!).


##### `non_rgb_theme_accent_remains_active_fallback`  (lines 79–98)

```
fn non_rgb_theme_accent_remains_active_fallback()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that when the theme accent is non-RGB, the palette falls back rather than attempting to blend unsupported colors. It also verifies that the active style’s bold modifier is preserved.

**Data flow**: Builds a palette with truecolor terminal capability but a bold `Color::Cyan` active style, then reads `for_level(1)` and `uses_color`, asserting the returned style equals the original active style, still contains `Modifier::BOLD`, and that `uses_color` is `false`.

**Call relations**: Invoked directly by the test runner. It exercises the `activity_anchor_rgb` failure path that forces `from_parts` into fallback mode.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_parts); 3 external calls (default, assert!, assert_eq!).


##### `missing_terminal_colors_use_theme_accent_fallback`  (lines 101–115)

```

**Purpose**: Verifies that absent terminal foreground/background RGB metadata also forces fallback palette construction. Active cells should still use the supplied accent style while empty cells remain dim.

**Data flow**: Passes `None` for `default_fg`, a valid background RGB, truecolor capability, and a bold blue active style into `from_parts`, then reads `for_level(0)`, `for_level(4)`, and `uses_color` to assert dim empty cells, accent-colored active cells, and `false` color-gradient mode.

**Call relations**: This direct unit test covers the missing-terminal-color early-return branch in `from_parts`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (from_parts); 3 external calls (default, assert!, assert_eq!).


### `tui/src/chatwidget/tokens/chart_tests.rs`

`test` · `request handling`

This test module exercises the pure chart-rendering helpers in `tokens/chart.rs`. Some tests target low-level transformations directly: `daily_values` must sum duplicate dates and clamp negative token counts to zero, `bar_levels` must fill bars from the bottom, and `TokenActivityView::parse` must accept documented aliases while rejecting unsupported values. These cases lock down the normalization and scaling rules that feed the renderer.

The remaining tests snapshot rendered chart text. They construct deterministic `NaiveDate` anchors and small sets of `AccountTokenUsageDailyBucket` values, then call `chart_lines` or `summary_lines`, convert the resulting `Line` values to trimmed strings, and compare them against inline snapshots. The snapshots verify several subtle layout properties: daily charts use distinct empty and active glyphs, wide terminals remain left-aligned rather than stretching oddly, weekly and cumulative modes render bar silhouettes with the correct captions, and summary metrics stay on one line when width permits but split cleanly when space is tight.

Because these tests operate below the async widget layer, they provide stable coverage for rendering semantics independent of request timing or UI state machines. They effectively document the expected textual shape of `/usage` output in terminals with the fallback glyph palette.

#### Function details

##### `duplicate_dates_sum_and_negative_values_clamp`  (lines 8–29)

```
fn duplicate_dates_sum_and_negative_values_clamp()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies the normalization rules in `daily_values`: duplicate dates are accumulated and negative token counts do not subtract from activity. The resulting total should reflect only nonnegative summed usage.

**Data flow**: Constructs a fixed `today` date and three daily buckets, including two for the same date and one negative-valued bucket for the previous day. It passes them to `daily_values`, sums the returned vector, and asserts the total is `15`.

**Call relations**: Run directly by the unit-test harness. It targets the data-normalization path that `chart_lines` depends on before any rendering occurs.

*Call graph*: 3 external calls (from_ymd_opt, assert_eq!, vec!).


##### `bar_levels_fill_from_bottom`  (lines 32–37)

```
fn bar_levels_fill_from_bottom()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that bar-chart occupancy is generated bottom-up rather than top-down. An empty week should produce all zeros, while a maximal week should fill every row.

**Data flow**: Calls `bar_levels(&[0, 10])`, then slices the returned flattened level vector into the first and second week segments and asserts the first is all zeros and the second is all fours.

**Call relations**: This direct unit test isolates `bar_levels`, which is later used by weekly and cumulative chart rendering.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (assert_eq!).


##### `token_activity_view_aliases_parse`  (lines 40–55)

```
fn token_activity_view_aliases_parse()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies the accepted `/usage` view aliases and the rejection of unsupported arguments. It documents the parser contract for empty, daily, weekly, cumulative, and invalid inputs.

**Data flow**: Calls `TokenActivityView::parse` with `""`, `"day"`, `"week"`, `"cumulative"`, and `"year"`, asserting the expected `Some(...)` or `None` result for each.

**Call relations**: Run directly by the test harness. It covers the command-argument parsing helper used by slash-command dispatch.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (assert_eq!).


##### `daily_graph_snapshot_uses_distinct_empty_and_active_cells`  (lines 58–91)

**Data flow**: Creates a fixed `today` date and two daily buckets, renders `chart_lines(TokenActivityView::Daily, &buckets, today, 22)`, converts each line to a trimmed string, joins them with newlines, and snapshots the final text.

**Call relations**: Invoked directly by the unit-test harness. It exercises the full daily rendering pipeline from bucket normalization through labels, glyph selection, legend, and footer.

*Call graph*: 3 external calls (from_ymd_opt, assert_snapshot!, vec!).


##### `daily_graph_snapshot_stays_left_aligned_in_wide_terminal`  (lines 94–112)

**Data flow**: First asserts `graph_width(160) == 107` and `graph_width(u16::MAX) == u16::MAX`. It then renders an empty daily chart at width 160, extracts the month-label line, first chart row, and footer, converts them to strings, joins them, and snapshots the result.

**Call relations**: This direct test covers both the width-calculation helper and the wide-layout branch of daily chart rendering.

##### `cumulative_graph_snapshot_renders_running_total_bar_chart_and_caption`  (lines 160–202)

fn cumulative_graph_snapshot_renders_running_total_bar_chart_and_caption()
```

**Purpose**: Snapshots a cumulative chart to verify running-total bar heights and the cumulative caption showing the top running total. It distinguishes cumulative scaling from plain weekly totals.

### `tui/src/chatwidget/tokens_tests.rs`

`test` · `request handling`

This small test module targets a subtle but important invariant in the token-activity state machine: once a usage response is marked loaded, the chart’s `today` anchor date must be frozen inside `TokenActivityState::Loaded`. That prevents the rendered 52-week window from drifting if the card is displayed later or inserted into history after a delay.

The test constructs the shared `Arc<RwLock<TokenActivityState>>` manually in `Loading` state, wraps it in a `TokenActivityHandle`, and then calls `finish_with_today` with a deterministic `NaiveDate` and a minimal `GetAccountTokenUsageResponse` whose summary fields are all `None`. After completion, it reads the lock and pattern-matches the resulting state. The assertion checks that the stored `loaded_today` exactly equals the supplied date; any other state triggers a panic with the unexpected debug representation.

Because this test bypasses the widget and async request-correlation layers, it isolates the state-transition contract of `TokenActivityHandle` itself. It documents that the completion path captures the date once and stores it in the loaded state, rather than deriving it lazily during rendering.

#### Function details

##### `loaded_state_freezes_chart_anchor_date_at_completion`  (lines 6–38)

```
fn loaded_state_freezes_chart_anchor_date_at_completion()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that `TokenActivityHandle::finish_with_today` stores the supplied completion date inside the loaded state. This ensures later rendering uses the original anchor date rather than the current date at display time.

**Data flow**: Creates an `Arc<RwLock<TokenActivityState::Loading>>`, clones it into a `TokenActivityHandle`, constructs a fixed `NaiveDate`, and calls `handle.finish_with_today(Ok(GetAccountTokenUsageResponse { ... }), today)`. It then reads the lock, matches on the resulting state, asserts the embedded `today` equals the expected date, and panics if the state is not `Loaded`.

**Call relations**: Run directly by the unit-test harness. It bypasses `TokenActivityHandle::finish` so the test can inject a deterministic date and inspect the exact state transition performed by `finish_with_today`.

*Call graph*: 6 external calls (clone, new, from_ymd_opt, new, assert_eq!, panic!).


### `tui/src/history_cell/tests.rs`

`test` · `cross-cutting`

This file is a dense test suite for the history-cell subsystem, built around concrete helper constructors from the parent module and adjacent subsystems such as `ExecCell`, MCP tool rendering, session info, and markdown rendering. It defines a few reusable fixtures: `test_config` builds an async `Config` rooted in a temp directory; `test_cwd` supplies a stable absolute cwd; TOML helpers synthesize `McpServerConfig` values for stdio and HTTP MCP servers; and rendering helpers flatten `ratatui::text::Line` values into plain strings so assertions can focus on visible output. The tests verify both semantic behavior and exact snapshots: preservation of explicit blank lines in raw source mode, markdown table upgrading in rich mode while keeping source intact in raw mode, hyperlink metadata surviving wrapping and composition, image/tool-call completion behavior, masking of sensitive MCP config values, and transcript formatting for session/tool/exec events. Several tests specifically guard invariants that are easy to regress: wrapped height must match actual rendered rows, URL-like tokens must not be split across lines, stale glyphs must be cleared when a cell shrinks, and consolidation logic must replace streamed `AgentMessageCell` runs with a single `AgentMarkdownCell`. The suite also covers platform-sensitive path rendering, timing labels, permission-profile “yolo mode” detection, and transcript replay stability after `word_wrap_lines`.

#### Function details

##### `test_config`  (lines 33–40)

```
async fn test_config() -> Config
```

**Purpose**: Builds a minimal asynchronous `Config` fixture rooted at the process temp directory. Tests use it when rendering depends on configuration flags or MCP server inventory.

**Data flow**: Reads `std::env::temp_dir()` as `codex_home`, feeds it into `ConfigBuilder::default().codex_home(...).build().await`, and returns the resulting `Config`. It panics if config construction fails.

**Call relations**: Invoked by async tests that need real config state, especially session-info and MCP inventory rendering cases. It delegates all actual config assembly to `ConfigBuilder` so tests exercise production deserialization/defaulting paths.

**Purpose**: Builds a test `McpServerConfig` representing a stdio-backed MCP server from simple Rust inputs. It lets tests describe command, args, env, and env-var passthroughs without hand-writing TOML.

**Call relations**: Called by MCP inventory tests that need realistic server config objects. It delegates map conversion to `string_map_to_toml_value` so both stdio and HTTP config helpers share the same TOML encoding.

### `tui/src/markdown_render_tests.rs`

`test` · `test execution`

This file is included into `markdown_render.rs` under `cfg(test)` and acts as the renderer’s behavioral specification. It defines two small helpers: `render_markdown_text_for_cwd`, which fixes a cwd for local-file-link tests, and `plain_lines`, which strips style metadata for easier assertions and snapshots. The tests are broad and concrete rather than synthetic.

A large group validates ordinary markdown semantics as rendered by the TUI: paragraphs, headings with exact style expectations, blockquotes across paragraphs and nesting, ordered/unordered lists including loose vs tight items, continuation paragraphs, nested mixed lists, inline formatting, links, HTML, horizontal rules, and code blocks with and without syntax highlighting. Several tests specifically target tricky interactions such as blockquotes inside list items, headings inside blockquotes, code blocks inside lists, and preservation of trailing blank lines inside fenced code.

Another major group focuses on local file links. These tests verify that local destinations hide the markdown label, decode percent-encoding, append line/range/hash suffixes from the target rather than the label, shorten only when under the provided cwd, and keep local-link targets inline with following `: description` text in wrapped list items.

The table tests exercise the renderer’s custom layout engine: themed bold headers, alignment markers, wrapped row separation, path-heavy columns, fallback to stacked key/value records under narrow widths, preservation of rich values and hyperlinks in fallback modes, escaped pipes, and blockquoted tables. Snapshot tests capture larger end-to-end outputs for complex markdown and width-sensitive table decisions.

#### Function details

##### `render_markdown_text_for_cwd`  (lines 18–20)

```
fn render_markdown_text_for_cwd(input: &str, cwd: &Path) -> Text<'static>
```

**Purpose**: Test helper that renders markdown with no width constraint but with a fixed cwd so local file-link display is deterministic.

**Data flow**: It takes input markdown and a `Path`, calls `render_markdown_text_with_width_and_cwd` with `None` width and `Some(cwd)`, and returns the resulting `Text<'static>`.

**Call relations**: Used by all local-file-link tests and snapshots so path shortening behavior does not depend on the process cwd.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (render_markdown_text_with_width_and_cwd); called by 12 (file_link_appends_hash_anchor_when_label_lacks_it, file_link_appends_hash_range_when_label_lacks_it, file_link_appends_line_number_when_label_lacks_it, file_link_appends_range_when_label_lacks_it, file_link_decodes_percent_encoded_bare_path_destination, file_link_hides_destination, file_link_keeps_absolute_paths_outside_cwd, file_link_uses_target_path_for_hash_anchor, file_link_uses_target_path_for_hash_range, file_link_uses_target_path_for_range (+2 more)).


##### `plain_lines`  (lines 22–32)

```
fn plain_lines(text: &Text<'_>) -> Vec<String>
```

**Purpose**: Converts rendered `Text` into plain strings for assertions and snapshots that ignore style metadata.

**Data flow**: It takes a `Text`, concatenates each line’s span contents into a `String`, and returns a `Vec<String>`.

**Call relations**: Shared by tests that compare rendered content shape rather than exact styled spans.

*Call graph*: called by 3 (list_item_after_code_block_keeps_blank_separator, outer_list_item_after_nested_code_block_keeps_blank_separator, table_key_value_fallback_preserves_rich_values_and_themed_labels).

### `tui/src/render/renderable_tests.rs`

`test` · `test execution`

This test file exists solely to exercise `FlexRenderable::allocate` with deterministic child heights. The local `HeightRenderable(u16)` wrapper is a minimal stand-in widget: its `render` method is intentionally empty, while `desired_height` returns the stored height unchanged. That lets the tests isolate allocation math from any drawing behavior.

The first test builds a flex container with two equally flexible children whose desired heights differ sharply (20 and 2) and allocates them into a `Rect` of height 10. The assertion checks that unused space from the short child is redistributed to the taller sibling, producing heights `[8, 2]` rather than a naive `[5, 5]`. The second test inserts a non-flex child between two flexible ones and confirms that fixed-height demand is reserved before flexible distribution occurs, yielding `[4, 2, 4]`. Both tests inspect only the returned `Rect` heights, not rendering output, which makes them precise regression tests for layout policy: fixed children consume their requested space first, and flexible children share only the remaining height, with redistribution when one child cannot use its nominal share.

#### Function details

##### `HeightRenderable::with_height`  (lines 7–9)

```
fn with_height(height: u16) -> Self
```

**Purpose**: Constructs a `HeightRenderable` carrying a fixed desired height for use in layout tests.

**Data flow**: Takes a `u16` height value, wraps it in `HeightRenderable`, and returns the new instance without touching external state.

**Call relations**: Both test cases call it while assembling `RenderableItem::Owned(Box<dyn Renderable>)` children, so the flex allocator sees predictable `desired_height` values.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (flex_redistributes_space_unused_by_short_children, flex_reserves_non_flex_space_before_flexible_children).


##### `HeightRenderable::render`  (lines 13–13)

```
fn render(&self, _area: Rect, _buf: &mut Buffer)
```

**Purpose**: Implements the `Renderable` trait's drawing hook as a no-op because these tests care only about sizing, not pixels.

**Data flow**: Receives a `Rect` and mutable `Buffer` but ignores both and returns unit, leaving the buffer unchanged.

**Call relations**: It satisfies the trait contract for boxed renderables used in the tests; no test logic depends on it being invoked.


##### `HeightRenderable::desired_height`  (lines 15–17)

```
fn desired_height(&self, _width: u16) -> u16
```

**Purpose**: Reports the fixed height stored in the test helper so allocation can be validated deterministically.

**Data flow**: Reads `self.0`, ignores the supplied width, and returns that `u16` as the desired height.

**Call relations**: This is the only meaningful behavior of the fake renderable and is what `FlexRenderable` consults during allocation in both tests.


##### `flex_redistributes_space_unused_by_short_children`  (lines 21–43)

```
fn flex_redistributes_space_unused_by_short_children()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that equal-flex children do not keep unused height when one child wants less than its nominal share.

**Data flow**: Creates a `FlexRenderable`, pushes two owned `HeightRenderable` children with flex 1 and heights 20 and 2, allocates them into a 10-row `Rect`, extracts each allocated area's `height`, and asserts the resulting vector is `[8, 2]`.

**Call relations**: This is a direct regression-style test of the allocator's redistribution path: after setup it delegates allocation to `FlexRenderable`, then checks the returned geometry.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (new, with_height); 4 external calls (new, new, assert_eq!, Owned).


##### `flex_reserves_non_flex_space_before_flexible_children`  (lines 46–72)

```
fn flex_reserves_non_flex_space_before_flexible_children()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that non-flex children consume their fixed space before remaining height is split among flexible siblings.

**Data flow**: Builds a `FlexRenderable` with three children—flex 1 / fixed 0 / flex 1—using desired heights 20, 2, and 20, allocates within a 10-row `Rect`, maps the resulting areas to heights, and asserts `[4, 2, 4]`.

**Call relations**: This test drives the allocator through the mixed fixed-plus-flex path, confirming the fixed middle child is reserved first and only the leftover rows are divided between the flexible children.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (new, with_height); 4 external calls (new, new, assert_eq!, Owned).


### `tui/src/status/tests.rs`

`test` · `test-only`

This file is the main behavioral test suite for status rendering. It does not define production logic; instead it assembles realistic `Config`, `PermissionProfile`, `TokenUsageInfo`, `RateLimitSnapshot`, and `RemoteConnectionStatus` inputs and drives the top-level status constructors such as `new_status_output`, `new_status_output_with_rate_limits`, and `new_status_output_with_rate_limits_handle`. Several small helpers keep the tests readable: `test_config` builds a config with managed-config loading disabled and a default workspace-write permission profile; `set_workspace_cwd` synchronizes `cwd`, `workspace_roots`, and permission roots; `token_info_for` derives a model context window from offline model metadata; `render_lines` flattens `ratatui::Line` values into plain strings; `sanitize_directory` rewrites the rendered directory field to `[[workspace]]` while preserving frame width for stable snapshots; and `reset_at_from` converts relative offsets into UTC epoch reset timestamps.

The tests cover a broad matrix of status concerns: reasoning-effort details, permission-profile labeling, workspace-root display, auto-review wording, provider-specific usage-link gating, fork ancestry, monthly and rolling rate limits, stale/missing/unavailable/refreshing limit states, credits visibility rules, cached-token omission, narrow-terminal truncation, and context-window accounting based on last-turn rather than total usage. Many assertions are snapshot-based, but several targeted tests extract a single rendered line or hyperlink destination list to verify exact semantics that would be easy to regress.

#### Function details

##### `stale_monthly_limit_marks_fresh_rolling_snapshot_stale`  (lines 55–80)

```
fn stale_monthly_limit_marks_fresh_rolling_snapshot_stale()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that an otherwise fresh rolling-window snapshot is classified as stale when its embedded monthly-limit capture time is too old.

**Data flow**: It builds a `RateLimitSnapshotDisplay` whose top-level `captured_at` is current but whose `individual_limit.captured_at` is 20 minutes old, passes it to `compose_rate_limit_data_many`, and asserts the result matches `StatusRateLimitData::Stale(_)`.

**Call relations**: This test directly targets the stale-propagation logic in rate-limit composition.

*Call graph*: 3 external calls (minutes, now, assert!).


##### `app_server_workspace_write_profile`  (lines 82–119)

```
fn app_server_workspace_write_profile(network_enabled: bool) -> PermissionProfile
```

**Purpose**: Constructs a managed permission profile representing workspace-write access plus root/tmp writable entries, with configurable network access.

**Data flow**: It takes a `bool` for network enablement and returns a `PermissionProfile::Managed` whose `file_system` is `ManagedFileSystemPermissions::Restricted` with explicit `FileSystemSandboxEntry` values for root read and project/tmp write access.

**Call relations**: It is used by `test_config` and by permission-label tests that need a non-default managed workspace profile.

*Call graph*: called by 2 (status_permissions_non_default_workspace_write_uses_workspace_label, test_config); 1 external calls (vec!).


##### `test_config`  (lines 121–136)

```
async fn test_config(temp_home: &TempDir) -> Config
```

**Purpose**: Builds a reusable test `Config` with managed-config loading disabled, user approvals, and a default workspace-write permission profile.

**Data flow**: It takes a temporary home directory, feeds it into `ConfigBuilder` with `LoaderOverrides::without_managed_config_for_tests()`, awaits config construction, mutates `approvals_reviewer`, installs the profile from `app_server_workspace_write_profile(true)`, and returns the config.

**Call relations**: This is the central fixture used by most tests in the file before they customize model, permissions, cwd, or provider settings.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (without_managed_config_for_tests, app_server_workspace_write_profile); called by 35 (status_card_token_usage_excludes_cached_tokens, status_context_window_uses_last_usage, status_model_provider_uses_bedrock_runtime_base_url_and_gates_usage_link, status_permissions_broadened_workspace_profile_shows_builtin_label, status_permissions_full_disk_managed_with_network_is_danger_full_access, status_permissions_full_disk_managed_without_network_is_external_sandbox, status_permissions_named_profile_shows_additional_writable_roots, status_permissions_named_read_only_profile_shows_builtin_label, status_permissions_named_workspace_profile_shows_builtin_label, status_permissions_non_default_workspace_write_uses_workspace_label (+15 more)); 2 external calls (path, default).


##### `set_workspace_cwd`  (lines 138–144)

```
fn set_workspace_cwd(config: &mut Config, cwd: AbsolutePathBuf)
```

**Purpose**: Synchronizes a config’s cwd and workspace roots with the permissions subsystem.

**Call relations**: It exercises interaction between remote-connection display and reasoning defaults in the top-level status constructor.

### Integration and VT100 smoke tests
These integration suites verify terminal-backed behavior, architectural boundaries, resize/reflow behavior, and VT100 history/live rendering in end-to-end or smoke-test form.

### `tui/tests/manager_dependency_regression.rs`

`test` · `test-time architectural regression checking`

This test file is a lightweight architecture guard. `rust_sources_under` recursively walks a directory tree starting from the resolved `src/chatwidget.rs` location, collecting every `.rs` file into a sorted `Vec<PathBuf>`. The main test then reads each source file as text and searches for a small forbidden string list: the concrete type names `AuthManager` and `ThreadManager`, plus the accessor-like call patterns `auth_manager(` and `thread_manager(`.

The test intentionally operates on source text rather than the compiled dependency graph, which makes it cheap and broad: any newly introduced direct reference in runtime TUI code will be caught regardless of whether it is exercised. It uses `codex_utils_cargo_bin::find_resource!` to locate the checked-in source tree in a runfiles-friendly way, derives the containing `src` directory, and scans everything beneath it. Violations are accumulated as human-readable strings of the form `<path> contains <needle>` and reported together in one assertion failure so a single run shows all regressions at once. The file opts into `#![allow(clippy::expect_used)]` because test setup intentionally uses `expect` for crisp failure messages when the source tree cannot be read.

#### Function details

##### `rust_sources_under`  (lines 6–20)

```
fn rust_sources_under(dir: &Path) -> Vec<PathBuf>
```

**Purpose**: Recursively enumerates Rust source files beneath a directory. It returns a sorted list of all `.rs` paths found in the subtree.

**Data flow**: Accepts `dir: &Path`, reads directory entries with `fs::read_dir`, iterates through them, recurses into subdirectories, pushes files whose extension is `rs` into a `Vec<PathBuf>`, sorts the vector in place, and returns it.

**Call relations**: Used only by the regression test to build the set of source files that will be scanned for forbidden manager references.

*Call graph*: called by 1 (tui_runtime_source_does_not_depend_on_manager_escape_hatches); 2 external calls (new, read_dir).


##### `tui_runtime_source_does_not_depend_on_manager_escape_hatches`  (lines 23–54)

```
fn tui_runtime_source_does_not_depend_on_manager_escape_hatches()
```

**Purpose**: Scans all runtime TUI source files and fails if any contain forbidden manager-related identifiers or accessor calls. It enforces that runtime code does not reach into manager escape hatches.

**Data flow**: Resolves `src/chatwidget.rs` via `find_resource!`, derives its parent directory, gathers all Rust sources with `rust_sources_under`, defines a fixed forbidden string array, reads each file’s contents, collects every matching `(path, needle)` into `violations: Vec<String>`, and asserts that the list is empty; on failure it prints all violations joined by newlines.

**Call relations**: This is the sole test entrypoint in the file. It orchestrates resource lookup, recursive file discovery, content scanning, and final assertion.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (rust_sources_under); 2 external calls (assert!, find_resource!).


### `tui/tests/suite/resize_reflow.rs`

`test` · `manual end-to-end regression testing for terminal resize behavior`

This file is an end-to-end regression suite for resize/reflow behavior that only runs manually (`#[ignore]`) because it requires tmux, a locally built `codex` binary, and network-enabled test conditions. Each scenario creates a temporary `CODEX_HOME`, writes a minimal trusted-project config and dummy auth file, starts a `wiremock::MockServer`, and mounts a canned SSE transcript whose assistant message is intentionally long enough to wrap across rows. The tests then launch Codex inside a detached tmux session with fixed dimensions and `--no-alt-screen`, wait until both the sentinel history text and model banner appear, type a draft into the composer, and capture pane contents to compute baseline row positions.

The three scenarios cover vertical split/reflow stability, repeated split/restore cycles, and horizontal width shrink/restore anchoring. Row positions are derived from pane captures: `last_composer_row` finds the last line whose trimmed content starts with the prompt glyph `›`, while `first_row_containing` locates the sentinel history line. Assertions compare baseline and post-resize rows and include full pane captures in failure messages for diagnosis.

Helper functions wrap tmux command execution with contextual errors and success checks, poll pane captures until expected text appears, and convert stdout bytes to strings. `TmuxSession` implements `Drop` to kill the tmux session automatically, ensuring cleanup even on failure. `codex_binary` first tries `cargo_bin("codex")` and then falls back to `codex-rs/target/debug/codex`, producing a targeted build hint if neither exists.

#### Function details

##### `tmux_split_preserves_fresh_session_composer_row_after_resize_reflow`  (lines 18–164)

```
async fn tmux_split_preserves_fresh_session_composer_row_after_resize_reflow() -> Result<()>
```

**Purpose**: Launches Codex in tmux, creates a vertical split beneath it, and verifies that the composer row does not drift during reflow or after the split is closed. It also checks that a known history line returns to the same row after restoration.

**Data flow**: Performs platform and environment guards, resolves the repo root and Codex binary, creates temp config/auth state, starts a mock SSE server, launches a detached tmux session running Codex, waits for sentinel text and model banner, sends a draft string into the pane, captures baseline pane contents, computes baseline composer and history rows, creates a vertical split pane, captures pane contents after split and after a delay, asserts row stability, kills the split pane, captures again, and asserts final composer/history rows match baseline before returning `Ok(())`.

**Call relations**: This is a top-level ignored async test. It orchestrates nearly all helpers in the file: fixture writers, SSE generation, tmux command wrappers, pane capture polling, and row-extraction utilities.

*Call graph*: calls 12 internal fn (mount_sse_once, capture_pane, check, checked_output, codex_binary, first_row_containing, last_composer_row, resize_reflow_sse, stdout_text, wait_for_capture_contains (+2 more)); 12 external calls (from_millis, from_secs, start, ensure!, cfg!, new, repo_root, eprintln!, format!, skip_if_no_network! (+2 more)).


##### `tmux_repeated_resizes_do_not_push_composer_down`  (lines 168–181)

```
async fn tmux_repeated_resizes_do_not_push_composer_down() -> Result<()>
```

**Purpose**: Runs the repeated-resize smoke scenario after performing the same platform, network, and tmux-availability guards as the other tests. Its goal is to ensure repeated split/restore cycles do not gradually push the composer downward.

**Data flow**: Checks for Windows, network availability, and tmux presence; if all prerequisites pass, awaits `run_repeated_resize_smoke()` and returns its `Result<()>`.

**Call relations**: This is a thin ignored async test wrapper whose only substantive delegation is to `run_repeated_resize_smoke`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (run_repeated_resize_smoke); 4 external calls (cfg!, new, eprintln!, skip_if_no_network!).


##### `tmux_width_resize_restore_keeps_visible_content_anchored`  (lines 185–312)

```
async fn tmux_width_resize_restore_keeps_visible_content_anchored() -> Result<()>
```

**Purpose**: Launches Codex in tmux, temporarily shrinks width by opening a horizontal split, then restores width and verifies both composer and history rows return to their baseline positions. It targets width-driven reflow anchoring rather than height-only changes.

**Data flow**: Performs the same setup as the vertical-split test, captures baseline composer and history rows after typing a draft, opens a horizontal split pane to reduce width, waits briefly, kills the split pane to restore width, captures the pane again, computes restored composer/history rows, and asserts both equal their baseline values before returning `Ok(())`.

**Call relations**: This is another top-level ignored async test that reuses the same helper stack as the first scenario but changes the tmux split orientation and assertions.

*Call graph*: calls 12 internal fn (mount_sse_once, capture_pane, check, checked_output, codex_binary, first_row_containing, last_composer_row, resize_reflow_sse, stdout_text, wait_for_capture_contains (+2 more)); 12 external calls (from_millis, from_secs, start, ensure!, cfg!, new, repo_root, eprintln!, format!, skip_if_no_network! (+2 more)).


##### `run_repeated_resize_smoke`  (lines 314–435)

```
async fn run_repeated_resize_smoke() -> Result<()>
```

**Purpose**: Implements the repeated split/restore scenario shared by the corresponding test wrapper. It verifies across three cycles that neither the composer row nor a sentinel history row drifts after the pane returns to its original size.

**Data flow**: Sets up repo root, Codex binary, temp home, mock SSE server, config/auth files, and a tmux session; launches Codex, waits for sentinel text and model banner, sends a draft, captures baseline pane contents and row positions, then loops three times creating a vertical split pane, sleeping briefly, killing the split pane, recapturing the main pane, recomputing composer/history rows, and asserting both match baseline on every cycle. Returns `Ok(())` if all cycles pass.

**Call relations**: Called only by `tmux_repeated_resizes_do_not_push_composer_down`. It is the shared orchestration body for that test’s repeated-resize assertions.

*Call graph*: calls 12 internal fn (mount_sse_once, capture_pane, check, checked_output, codex_binary, first_row_containing, last_composer_row, resize_reflow_sse, stdout_text, wait_for_capture_contains (+2 more)); called by 1 (tmux_repeated_resizes_do_not_push_composer_down); 9 external calls (from_millis, from_secs, start, ensure!, new, repo_root, format!, sleep, tempdir).

##### `wait_for_capture_contains`  (lines 496–508)

```
fn wait_for_capture_contains(pane: &str, needle: &str, timeout: Duration) -> Result<String>
```

**Purpose**: Polls a tmux pane until its captured text contains a target substring or a timeout expires. It returns the successful capture so callers can inspect row positions immediately.

**Data flow**: Accepts a pane id, search needle, and timeout `Duration`, computes a deadline from `Instant::now() + timeout`, repeatedly captures the pane with `capture_pane`, checks `contains(needle)`, sleeps 100 ms between attempts, and either returns the matching capture or bails with an error containing the last capture.

**Call relations**: Used by the smoke scenarios to wait for startup text and typed draft text before making row-position assertions.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (capture_pane); called by 3 (run_repeated_resize_smoke, tmux_split_preserves_fresh_session_composer_row_after_resize_reflow, tmux_width_resize_restore_keeps_visible_content_anchored); 5 external calls (from_millis, now, new, bail!, sleep).

```

**Purpose**: Captures the visible contents of a tmux pane as a UTF-8-lossy `String`. It is the basic observation primitive for all row-position checks.

**Data flow**: Builds `tmux capture-pane -p -t <pane>`, executes it through `output`, converts `output.stdout` with `String::from_utf8_lossy`, and returns the resulting owned string.

### `tui/tests/suite/status_indicator.rs`

`test` · `test-time regression check for ANSI sanitization`

This file is intentionally tiny and tests one specific rendering invariant: status-indicator text must not leak raw ANSI escape bytes into the backing buffer. Rather than trying to exercise the full widget rendering pipeline, it targets the public helper `codex_ansi_escape::ansi_escape_line`, which the widget relies on internally.

The test constructs a string containing the ANSI SGR sequence for red text (`\x1b[31mRED\x1b[0m`), passes it through `ansi_escape_line`, then concatenates the returned `ratatui` spans back into a plain `String`. The assertion checks that the visible content is exactly `RED`, which simultaneously proves that the printable glyphs were preserved and that the escape sequences themselves were stripped rather than emitted as literal bytes. The module-level comment explains this choice of scope: end-to-end widget rendering is awkward to test directly, so the regression is pinned to the helper’s public behavior instead.

#### Function details

##### `ansi_escape_line_strips_escape_sequences`  (lines 10–24)

```
fn ansi_escape_line_strips_escape_sequences()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that `ansi_escape_line` removes ANSI escape sequences from colored input while preserving the visible text content. It is a regression test for status-indicator buffer safety.

**Data flow**: Creates an ANSI-colored input string, passes it to `ansi_escape_line`, concatenates all returned span contents into a `String`, and asserts that the combined text equals `RED`.

**Call relations**: This is the sole test entrypoint in the file and directly exercises the public helper the status indicator depends on.

*Call graph*: 2 external calls (assert_eq!, ansi_escape_line).


### `tui/tests/suite/vt100_history.rs`

`test` · `test-time verification of VT100 history rendering behavior`

This test file validates how committed history lines are rendered into a `codex_tui::Terminal<VT100Backend>`. The local `TestScenario` helper constructs a terminal with a synthetic VT100 backend of a chosen size, sets a viewport rectangle, and exposes `run_insert` to feed `Vec<Line<'static>>` into `codex_tui::insert_history_lines`. That keeps each test focused on observable terminal state rather than setup boilerplate.

The assertions inspect the backend’s reconstructed screen contents or terminal cursor state after insertion. Coverage includes simple multi-line insertion without wrapping, preservation of all characters when a long token wraps across rows, visibility of emoji and CJK characters, concatenation of mixed styled/plain spans, and restoration of `last_known_cursor_pos` after history insertion. Two prose-focused regressions specifically ensure wrapping does not split words mid-token: one for ordinary English text and one for a sentence containing an em dash before the word `inside`.

A small `assert_contains!` macro improves failure messages when checking that screen contents include expected strings. The tests use fixed viewport placement at the bottom row so inserted history scrolls above the live input area, matching the production layout assumptions that previously regressed.

#### Function details

##### `TestScenario::new`  (lines 27–33)

```
fn new(width: u16, height: u16, viewport: Rect) -> Self
```

**Purpose**: Builds a VT100-backed terminal test harness with a specified screen size and viewport area. It centralizes the terminal construction used by all tests in the file.

**Data flow**: Accepts `width`, `height`, and a `Rect` viewport, constructs `VT100Backend::new(width, height)`, creates `codex_tui::Terminal::with_options(backend)`, sets the viewport area on the terminal, and returns `TestScenario { term }`.

**Call relations**: Called by every test in the file before inserting history lines.

*Call graph*: calls 2 internal fn (with_options, new); called by 7 (basic_insertion_no_wrap, cursor_restoration, em_dash_and_space_word_wrap, emoji_and_cjk, long_token_wraps, mixed_ansi_spans, word_wrap_no_mid_word_split).


##### `TestScenario::run_insert`  (lines 35–38)

```
fn run_insert(&mut self, lines: Vec<Line<'static>>)
```

**Purpose**: Inserts a batch of history lines into the test terminal and fails immediately if insertion errors. It is the harness method all tests use to exercise history rendering.

**Data flow**: Takes `&mut self` and `Vec<Line<'static>>`, passes them to `codex_tui::insert_history_lines(&mut self.term, lines)`, and unwraps success with `expect`.

**Call relations**: Invoked by each scenario test after constructing a `TestScenario`.

*Call graph*: 1 external calls (insert_history_lines).


##### `basic_insertion_no_wrap`  (lines 42–54)

```
fn basic_insertion_no_wrap()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that two short history lines are inserted and visible on the VT100 screen without wrapping artifacts. It is the baseline history insertion test.

**Data flow**: Creates a 20x6 terminal with a one-row viewport at the bottom, inserts `first` and `second`, reads the screen contents string, and asserts both substrings are present.

**Call relations**: Uses `TestScenario::new` and `run_insert` to validate the simplest insertion path.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (new, assert_contains!, vec!).


##### `long_token_wraps`  (lines 57–86)

```
fn long_token_wraps()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that a long unbroken token wraps across rows without losing characters. It counts rendered `A` cells rather than relying on exact line breaks.

**Data flow**: Creates a long 45-character `A` string, inserts it into a 20-column terminal, iterates over all screen cells, counts cells whose first character is `A`, and asserts the count equals the original string length.

**Call relations**: Exercises history insertion and wrapping behavior under overflow conditions.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (new, assert_eq!, vec!).


##### `emoji_and_cjk`  (lines 89–105)

```
fn emoji_and_cjk()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that wide Unicode characters such as emoji and CJK glyphs survive insertion into the VT100 screen. It ignores whitespace and verifies each visible character appears somewhere in the reconstructed contents.

**Data flow**: Builds a string containing emoji and Chinese characters, inserts it, obtains the screen contents, iterates over non-whitespace source characters, and asserts each is contained in the rendered output.

**Call relations**: Validates Unicode rendering through the history insertion path.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 4 external calls (new, from, assert!, vec!).


##### `mixed_ansi_spans`  (lines 108–118)

```
fn mixed_ansi_spans()
```

**Purpose**: Ensures a line composed of styled and unstyled spans is inserted as contiguous visible text. It checks that style boundaries do not corrupt textual output.

**Data flow**: Creates a line from a red `red` span plus plain `+plain`, inserts it, reads screen contents, and asserts the combined string `red+plain` is present.

**Call relations**: Exercises history insertion with multi-span `Line` input.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (new, assert_contains!, vec!).


##### `cursor_restoration`  (lines 121–130)

```
fn cursor_restoration()
```

**Purpose**: Verifies that inserting history lines restores the terminal’s last known cursor position. This guards against history rendering disturbing the live input cursor.

**Data flow**: Creates a scenario, inserts a single `x` line, then asserts `scenario.term.last_known_cursor_pos` equals `(0, 0).into()`.

**Call relations**: Uses the harness to validate cursor-state preservation after `insert_history_lines`.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (new, assert_eq!, vec!).


##### `word_wrap_no_mid_word_split`  (lines 133–147)

```
fn word_wrap_no_mid_word_split()
```

**Purpose**: Checks that ordinary prose inserted into history does not split words across lines mid-token. It specifically guards against wrapping `both` as `bo\nth`.

**Data flow**: Inserts a long English paragraph into a 40-column terminal, reads the full screen contents, and asserts the joined contents do not contain the substring `bo\nth`.

**Call relations**: Exercises the history insertion path’s wrapping behavior for normal prose.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (new, assert!, vec!).


##### `em_dash_and_space_word_wrap`  (lines 150–164)

```
fn em_dash_and_space_word_wrap()
```

**Purpose**: Regression test ensuring wrapping around an em dash still breaks before the following word rather than inside it. It specifically guards against splitting `inside` as `insi\nde`.

**Data flow**: Inserts a sentence containing `sand—and inside`, reads the screen contents, and asserts the joined contents do not contain `insi\nde`.

**Call relations**: Uses the same harness as the other tests to pin a previously reported wrapping bug.

*Call graph*: calls 1 internal fn (new); 3 external calls (new, assert!, vec!).


### `tui/tests/suite/vt100_live_commit.rs`

`test` · `test-time verification of live-row overflow commit behavior`

This file contains a single integration-style test for the boundary between the live row ring and committed history. It constructs a `VT100Backend` terminal with a one-row viewport at the bottom, then uses `codex_tui::RowBuilder::new(20)` to append five explicit newline-terminated fragments: `one`, `two`, `three`, `four`, and `five`.

The key operation is `drain_commit_ready(/*max_keep*/ 3)`, which is expected to keep the last three rows in the live ring and return the older overflow rows as commit-ready history. The test converts those returned rows into `Vec<Line<'static>>` by taking each row’s `text` field and turning it into a `Line`, then inserts them with `codex_tui::insert_history_lines`.

Finally, it inspects the VT100 screen contents and asserts that `one` and `two` are visible above the viewport. The comment documents the intended invariant: `three`, `four`, and `five` remain live and are not committed by this test. This makes the test a narrow regression check that overflowed live rows are not lost when transitioned into history.

#### Function details

##### `live_001_commit_on_overflow`  (lines 6–43)

```
fn live_001_commit_on_overflow()
```

**Purpose**: Builds five rows in a live row builder, commits the overflow beyond the last three, inserts those committed rows into history, and verifies the earliest rows are visible on the VT100 screen. It checks the handoff from live-ring overflow to committed history.

**Data flow**: Constructs a VT100 terminal and viewport, creates `RowBuilder::new(20)`, pushes five newline-terminated fragments, calls `drain_commit_ready(3)` to obtain commit-ready rows, maps each row’s `text` into `Line<'static>`, inserts them with `insert_history_lines`, reads the screen contents, and asserts that `one` and `two` are present.

**Call relations**: This is the sole test in the file and directly exercises `RowBuilder` overflow behavior together with history insertion.

*Call graph*: calls 3 internal fn (with_options, new, new); 3 external calls (new, assert!, insert_history_lines).
